JPN 196.name: Independent Study
JPN 196.prerequisites: Consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.
JPN 196.description: Individual study under faculty direction for qualified students with special interests and needs. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 8 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.

JPN 200.name: Advanced Spoken Japanese
JPN 200.prerequisites: JPN 104; or consent of the instructor.
JPN 200.description: Emphasis on advanced oral and aural proficiency. Expression of cultural and personal topics using formal and informal language. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): JPN 104; or consent of the instructor.

JPN 210.name: Advanced Japanese Language and Culture
JPN 210.description: Expansion of communication skills, especially speaking, through an exploration of contemporary Japanese culture. Course Information: Grade of C or better in JPN 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

JST 101.name: Introduction to Judaism
JST 101.description: Introduction to the Jewish religion as it has developed from its roots in the Hebrew Bible until today, emphasizing its central texts and concepts. Same as RELS 121. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

JST 102.name: Introduction to Jewish History
JST 102.description: Overview of Jewish history from Biblical times until the present. Topics will range from second Temple Judea and the beginning of the diaspora through 21st-century Jewish life in America and Israel. Course Information: Same as RELS 126. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE Past course.

JST 103.name: Introduction to Israel Studies
JST 103.description: Introduction to major themes, issues, writers and contexts of the founding, establishment, and conflicts of the modern state of Israel. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 117.name: Understanding the Holocaust
JST 117.description: Examines the Holocaust of European Jewry as the result of antisemitic ideology and the development of modern German political forces; implementation of the Final Solution. Course Information: Same as HIST 117 and RELS 117. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE Past course.

JST 122.name: Minority Perspectives in the Germanic Context
JST 122.description: Investigation of the challenges and/or opportunities of multicultural societies by examining in a socio-historical context texts created by members of Europe's ethnic, religious, and national minorities. Course Information: Same as GER 122 and RELS 122. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Lectures, discussion, and readings in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 123.name: Introduction to Yiddish Culture and Literature
JST 123.description: Yiddish culture in Europe and the U.S. in socio-historical context. Focus on the role of Yiddish in conceptions of secular, cultural, religious, national Jewish identities. Course Information: Same as GER 123 and RELS 123. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Lectures, discussion, and readings in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 124.name: Introduction to the Hebrew Bible
JST 124.description: A study of the Five Books of Moses (a.k.a Torah or Pentateuch) within the contexts of the ancient Near East and biblical literature.  Course Information:  Same as CL 124 and RELS 124.  Taught in English. UIC GE Past course.

JST 125.name: Diaspora, Exile, Genocide: Aspects of the European Jewish Experience in Literature and Film
JST 125.description: Literature and films on European Jewish responses to anti-Semitism and persecution in a historical context to reveal the condition of post-Enlightenment German-speaking Jewish and Yiddish-speaking societies. Course Information: Same as GER 125 and RELS 127. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Taught in English. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 203.name: Israel Film: Aspects of History, Life, and Culture
JST 203.description: Exploration of major themes related to life in Israel via its national cinema. Historical, social, and cultural aspects of Israeli society are examined through film. UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 220.name: Modern Polish-Jewish Culture and Literatures
JST 220.prerequisites: ENGL 160 or ENGL 161. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
JST 220.description: Polish-Jewish literature and non-fiction works in English translation from Polish, Yiddish and Hebrew are studied as expressions of a distinct Ashkenazi or East European Jewish culture that developed on historically Polish lands. Course Information: Same as POL 220 and CEES 220. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 160 or ENGL 161. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 225.name: Topics in Muslim-Jewish Relations
JST 225.description: Muslim-Jewish interactions from the rise of Islam until contemporary times, the relationship between Biblical and Quranic materials, the Jewish and Islamic interpretive tradition and the legal systems of the two religious traditions.  Course Information:  Same as CL 225 and RELS 225.

JST 230.name: Topics in Jewish Thought
JST 230.description: Survey of major topics in Jewish theology, philosophy, ethics and political thought. Course Information: Same as RELS 231. May be repeated if topics vary. Recommended Background: JST 101 or the equivalent.

JST 240.name: Philosophy and Revelation: Jewish and Christian Perspectives
JST 240.prerequisites: One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
JST 240.description: Introduction to philosophical ways of addressing the claim that a book (the Bible, the Quran) comes from God.  Texts by Immanuel Kant, Moses Mendelssohn, and Soren Kierkegaard, among others.  Course Information:  Previously listed as JST 141. Same as RELS 240 and PHIL 240.  Prerequisite(s): One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 242.name: The History of Jewish Biblical Interpretation
JST 242.description: Jewish interpretation of the Hebrew bible. A survey of the span of Jewish history and the wide range of cultural contexts that have impacted the understanding of the Torah.  Course Information:  Same as CL 242 and RELS 242. UIC GE Past course.

JST 243.name: Politics and Government of the Middle East
JST 243.prerequisites: POLS 130 or POLS 190; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE World Cultures course.
JST 243.description: Contemporary Middle East political institutions, culture, processes, and conflicts. Emphasis on interaction of traditional and modern forces, such as Islam, nationalism, political elites, ideologies, states. Course Information: Same as POLS 243. Prerequisite(s): POLS 130 or POLS 190; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE World Cultures course.

JST 296.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
JST 296.description: How Jews became a modern ethnic group, how their experiences compare with other ethnic groups, and how their experiences in modern times vary from nation to nation. Course Information: Same as RELS 296. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Previously listed as JST 294.

JST 311.name: Gender and Sexuality in Early Christianity and Judaism
JST 311.description: Examination of the root of contemporary perspectives on gender and sexuality in the early traditions of Judaism and Christianity including the Bible, the Epic of Gilgamesh, the Church Fathers, the Talmud, and legends of the saints.  Course Information:  Same as GWS 311 and RELS 311.

JST 394.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
JST 394.description: Selected topics in Jewish culture and history. Course Information: Same as RELS 395. May be repeated to a maximum of 9 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor.

JST 410.name: Advanced Studies in Jewish Literature, Art and Culture
JST 410.prerequisites: One course in Jewish Studies. Recommended background: JST 102.
JST 410.description: Selected topics in Jewish literature and/or arts, in one or more places and time periods. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated up to 1 time(s) if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): One course in Jewish Studies. Recommended background: JST 102.

JST 420.name: Advanced Studies in Jewish Religion
JST 420.prerequisites: JST 101; and JST 230 or JST 242 or JST 254.
JST 420.description: In-depth study of a period or mode of Jewish religious development or textual production, or an examination of a religious tenet or practice across various historical periods. Topics will vary.Course Information: Same as RELS 420. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated up to 1 time(s) if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): JST 101; and JST 230 or JST 242 or JST 254.

JST 430.name: Advanced Studies in Jewish Thought
JST 430.prerequisites: One 100-level and one 200-level course in Jewish Studies.
JST 430.description: Advanced topics in Jewish theology, philosophy, ethics or political thought. Course Information: Same as RELS 430. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): One 100-level and one 200-level course in Jewish Studies.

JST 475.name: The Bible as Literature: Hebrew Bible
JST 475.description: Literary analysis of genres and themes of the Hebrew Bible and close reading of the biblical texts. Sources of the Bible and their historical context. Course Information: Same as ENGL 475 and RELS 475. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as JST 478. Taught in English. Recommended background: Any of ENGL 175 or ENGL 207-209.

JST 494.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
JST 494.prerequisites: JST 101 or JST 102 or consent of the instructor.
JST 494.description: Selected topics in Jewish studies. Course Information: Same as RELS 494. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours if topics vary.  Prerequisite(s): JST 101 or JST 102 or consent of the instructor.

KN 100.name: Kinesiology: First Year Seminar
KN 100.description: Overview of kinesiology emphasizing the key concepts of the sub-disciplines and diverse range of careers in the field. Focuses on academic and professional preparation given the integrative nature of the discipline and current trends.

KN 110.name: Life Skills for Student Athletes
KN 110.description: Prepares student-athletes, and those working with student-athletes, to thrive as leaders in their community and workplace, be productive members of society, and contribute value to their own life and to those with whom they interact.

KN 120.name: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
KN 120.description: An introduction to human anatomy and physiology, including cellular structures and selected organ systems, utilizing scientific processes and laboratory demonstration. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Laboratory.

KN 130.name: Stress Management
KN 130.description: Introduction to stress and its effects on health, with experiential application of coping strategies and relaxation techniques. Addresses conventional and innovative approaches, with a special emphasis on holistic health and wellness.

KN 136.name: Techniques and Principles of Exercise
KN 136.description: Teaches students how to identify, describe, execute, and progress common resistance and aerobic exercises for optimal health, athletic performance and overall fitness. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 137.name: Personal Fitness
KN 137.description: Evaluation of each student's level of fitness, followed by participation in a group exercise program. Variable training modes. Discussion on fitness-related topics.

KN 150.name: The Healthy Human
KN 150.description: Using the core dimensions of wellness, this course prepares students to live healthy, proactive lifestyles by creating a personal framework to serve them in their daily lives. Emphasis will be on the holistic integration of body, mind, and spirit. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

KN 151.name: Success Strategies for Anatomy and Physiology
KN 151.description: Teaches students to engage in effective study and learning strategies, with an emphasis on metacognition, for success in A&P. These metacognitive skills are transferable to other courses in the student's higher education experience. Course Information: Corequisites: Requires concurrent registration in KN 230 or KN 253.

KN 152.name: Introduction to Exercise Physiology and Health
KN 152.description: Provides students with the fundamental knowledge of the structure and function of the human body, particularly as it relates to the interaction between physical activity and health and disease. Course Information: Recommended background: High school chemistry, biology and/or physiology. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory-Discussion and one Lecture-Discussion. UIC GE Natural World Lab course.

KN 194.name: Special Topics in Kinesiology
KN 194.description: Participation and study in selected activities in Kinesiology. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Students may register in more than one section per term.

KN 200.name: Statistical Methods
KN 200.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in MATH 110. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion.
KN 200.description: How to apply, interpret, and think critically about scientific data from the biological and health sciences. Extensive hands-on experience working with data to illustrate concepts and develop skills for decision making and analytical thinking. Course Information: Credit is not given for KN 200 if the student has credit in STAT 101. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in MATH 110. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion.

KN 230.name: Anatomy and Physiology Lecture I
KN 230.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in BIOS 110 and cumulative undergraduate GPA of at least 2.5 (A=4.0).
KN 230.description: The structure and function of mammalian cells and tissues and human skeletal, muscular and nervous systems are discussed. Integrating the functions of the various systems is emphasized. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Credit for KN 230 is not given if the student has credit in KN 251 or KN 253. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in BIOS 110 and cumulative undergraduate GPA of at least 2.5 (A=4.0).

KN 231.name: Anatomy and Physiology Lecture II
KN 231.prerequisites: KN 230. Credit for KN 231 is not given if the student has credit in KN 252 or KN 254.
KN 231.description: The structure and function of the human endocrine, circulatory, respiratory, digestive, sensory, and reproductive systems are discussed.  Integrating the functions of the various systems is emphasized. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Prerequisite(s): KN 230. Credit for KN 231 is not given if the student has credit in KN 252 or KN 254.

KN 232.name: Anatomy and Physiology Laboratory I
KN 232.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in BIOS 110 and Credit or concurrent registration in KN 230 or the equivalent and GPA 2.5.
KN 232.description: Course to complement KN 230. Includes wet and dry lab activities. Human cadavers used in instruction. Course Information: Credit for KN 232 is not given if the student has credit in KN 251 or KN 255. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in BIOS 110 and Credit or concurrent registration in KN 230 or the equivalent and GPA 2.5.

KN 233.name: Anatomy and Physiology Laboratory II
KN 233.prerequisites: Credit or concurrent registration in KN 231 or the equivalent.
KN 233.description: Course to complement KN 231. Includes wet and dry lab activities. Human cadavers used in instruction. Course Information: Credit for KN 233 is not given if the student has credit in KN 252 or KN 256.Prerequisite(s): Credit or concurrent registration in KN 231 or the equivalent.

KN 237.name: Sport and Exercise Psychology
KN 237.description: Presents the psychological basis for exercise and sport motivation and behavior. Focus on application of theoretical models of exercise and sport and psychological strategies to improve participation in exercise and improve sport performance. Course Information: Previously listed as KN 335. Credit is not given for KN 237 if the student has credit in KN 335.

KN 243.name: Exercise Assessment
KN 243.prerequisites: KN 136. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 243.description: This introductory-level course addresses screening and assesses fitness components necessary to assess posture, body composition, strength, flexibility and cardio-respiratory endurance. Course Information: Extensive use of instrumentation. Prerequisite(s): KN 136. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 245.name: Exercise Programming and Instruction
KN 245.prerequisites: KN 136 and KN 243. Course Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 245.description: Emphasizes how to develop and instruct individual and group exercise programs utilizing a variety of exercise modalities. Personal training, small group instruction, and group exercise techniques are included in both lecture and lab experiences. Course Information: Credit is not given for KN 245 if the student has credit in KN 240. Prerequisite(s): KN 136 and KN 243. Course Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 246.name: Group Exercise Instruction
KN 246.prerequisites: KN 245. Course Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 246.description: Focuses on group fitness fundamentals for apparently healthy exercisers. Emphasis will be on proper exercise instruction, cuing, class organization, safety, and effective exercise planning for a variety of different class types. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 245. Course Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 261.name: Applied Musculoskeletal Anatomy
KN 261.description: Designed to provide a foundational knowledge base regarding the structure of the human musculoskeletal system as it relates to movement and function. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 251 or KN 253 or KN 230.

KN 262.name: Training Methods for Core Stability
KN 262.prerequisites: Junior standing or above; and consent of the instructor. Recommended background: KN 261. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 262.description: Students learn methods to assess and program core stability for a variety of populations in the contexts of posture, endurance, strength and flexibility. Extensive use of Pilates equipment and small apparatus. Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above; and consent of the instructor. Recommended background: KN 261. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 294.name: Special Topics in Kinesiology
KN 294.description: Selected topics in Kinesiology. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Students may register in more than one section per term.

KN 299.name: Study Abroad in Kinesiology and Nutrition
KN 299.description: Provides credit for study abroad. Student's proposal must have prior approval of the student's major program or department. Final determination of credit is made on the student's completion of the work. Course Information: May be repeated for a maximum of 36 hours.

KN 300.name: Research Methods and Inquiry in Kinesiology
KN 300.description: Introduces undergraduate students to inquiry processes and research methods applied in the field of Kinesiology. Course Information: Recommended background: KN 200 or PSCH 242.

KN 331.name: Sport and Exercise Injury Management
KN 331.prerequisites: KN 261. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 331.description: Fundamental management of exercise and sport related injuries and conditions.  Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 261. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 336.name: Health Coaching
KN 336.description: Behavior change approaches with diverse patients/clients using foundational health coaching and motivational principles. Students use communication, coaching and comportment skills to help guide others in the pursuit of healthy lifestyle habits. Course Information: Previously listed as KN 436. Credit is not given for KN 336 if the student has credit for KN 436. Prerequisite(s): KN 237.

KN 337.name: Psychology of Injury and Recovery
KN 337.description: Introduces the psychological, social, and emotional experiences associated with the acquisition and experience of physical injuries. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 237.

KN 345.name: Exercise Assessment and Programming
KN 345.prerequisites: KN 243, and KN 245, and either KN 251, or KN 230 and KN 232. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 345.description: Emphasizes a variety of advanced experiences in integrating assessment and programming techniques and approaches to exercise, fitness, health and sport. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 243, and KN 245, and either KN 251, or KN 230 and KN 232. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 350.name: Cadaver Dissection I
KN 350.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KN 251 or Grade of C or better in KN 255 or Grade of C or better in KN 232; or consent of the instructor.
KN 350.description: Cadaver dissection using the regional approach. Dissection of the musculo-skeletal system, spinal cord and peripheral nervous system. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KN 251 or Grade of C or better in KN 255 or Grade of C or better in KN 232; or consent of the instructor.

KN 351.name: Cadaver Dissection II
KN 351.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KN 252 or Grade of C or better in KN 256 or Grade of C or better in KN 233; or consent of the instructor.
KN 351.description: Cadaver dissection using the regional approach method. Dissection of the brain, cardiovascular, respiratory, digestive, urinary and reproductive systems. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KN 252 or Grade of C or better in KN 256 or Grade of C or better in KN 233; or consent of the instructor.

KN 352.name: Physiology of Exercise
KN 352.prerequisites: KN 152; and KN 231 or KN 252 or KN 254. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 352.description: The physiological responses associated with acute and chronic physical exercise; muscular, circulatory, respiratory and nervous systems. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 152; and KN 231 or KN 252 or KN 254. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 361.name: Movement Biomechanics
KN 361.prerequisites: MATH 121 and KN 261 and PHYS 131 or higher.
KN 361.description: Introduces students to the science of mechanics with a particular emphasis on the application of mechanics to the analysis of normal and pathological human and animal movement. Course Information: Previously listed as KN 260. Prerequisite(s): MATH 121 and KN 261 and PHYS 131 or higher.

KN 362.name: Tissue Biomechanics
KN 362.description: Introduces students to the biomechanics of the tissues of the body, emphasizing the musculoskeletal system. Students will study the structure-function relationship of tissues, and how this relationship is disrupted with disease or injury. Course Information: Credit is not given for KN 362 if the student has credit in KN 465. Prerequisite(s): MATH 121; and PHYS 131.

KN 372.name: Motor Control and Learning
KN 372.description: Introduction to basic principles regarding the acquisition and control of human movements. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 252; or KN 231 and KN 233.

KN 393.name: Undergraduate Internship in Kinesiology
KN 393.prerequisites: Approval of the department and completion of all required courses for the chosen internship site.
KN 393.description: This course will provide students with a working experience at a professional job site where they can apply the knowledge, skills and abilities they have learned in the program.  Course Information:  Field work required.  Students must provide their own transportation to and from internship sites. Prerequisite(s):  Approval of the department and completion of all required courses for the chosen internship site.

KN 394.name: Special Topics in Kinesiology
KN 394.prerequisites: Sophomore standing or above; and consent of the instructor.
KN 394.description: Flexible course structure designed to accommodate additional topics not covered in current course offerings. Topic examples include muscle physiology, psychology of physical activity, biomechanics and motor control of special populations. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore standing or above; and consent of the instructor.

KN 396.name: Special Project
KN 396.prerequisites: Approval of student project by the KN 396 instructor and supervising instructor.
KN 396.description: Selected topics in Kinesiology for individual study. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Prerequisite(s): Approval of student project by the KN 396 instructor and supervising instructor.

KN 397.name: Special Project Presentation
KN 397.description: Students will prepare and present their experiential learning project in a public forum. Emphasis will be on building the competencies of scientific communication and professionalism. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 396.

KN 398.name: Undergraduate Research
KN 398.description: An in-depth research analysis for the development of a research proposal in the student's area of interest. Review current literature, investigate various research methodologies, review the relevant research policies, and develop a proposed project. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Approval of the department.

KN 399.name: Undergraduate Research Presentation
KN 399.description: The implementation of the proposal developed in KN 398. Data collection, analysis and interpretation will provide the basis for the written project. The project will be presented in an open forum to faculty and other students. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 398.

KN 400.name: Entrepreneurship for Applied Health Professionals
KN 400.description: Relates the theory, principles and practices applied in entrepreneurial start-up settings in healthcare and human performance professions. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above.

KN 410.name: Aging and the Motor System
KN 410.description: Introduction to aging with a focus on its impact on the physical structure and function of the neural, muscular and skeletal systems; the mechanics through which the trajectory of aging can be potentially modified. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 252; or KN 231 and KN 233.

KN 431.name: Lower Extremity Overuse Injury
KN 431.prerequisites: KN 261 and KN 331. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 431.description: Critical review of the literature related to lower extremity overuse injury; current practices and research gaps in the prevention and treatment of these injuries; movement assessment and corrective exercise to prevent and care of these injuries. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 261 and KN 331. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 432.name: Foundations of Positive Psychology
KN 432.prerequisites: Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.
KN 432.description: Introduces students to positive psychology and the opportunities for human development channeling positive thoughts, emotions and behaviors to achieve life, relationships and legacy goals. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.

KN 433.name: Sociocultural Perspectives in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology
KN 433.prerequisites: Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.
KN 433.description: Pertinent sociological issues and theories will be examined in the scholarly pursuit of knowledge and understanding of the sociological and cultural influences in the field of performance, sport and exercise psychology. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.

KN 434.name: Business Branding in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology
KN 434.prerequisites: Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.
KN 434.description:
Designed to help students build a coaching and/or consulting business for face-to-face and virtual clients. Course Information: Prerequisite(s):
Acceptance into the concentration in Performance, Sport and Exercise Psychology within the MS in Kinesiology or consent of the instructor.

KN 435.name: Sport Psychology for Individual and Team Performance
KN 435.description: Analysis and application of psychological concepts related to process and outcomes of sport and exercise programs. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 237 or KN 335.

KN 438.name: Advanced Sport and Exercise Psychology
KN 438.prerequisites: KN 237. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 438.description: Explores research on the psychological effects of sport and exercise across the lifespan. Emphasis will be placed on understanding research methods and applying knowledge of consequences in practice. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 237. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 441.name: Muscle Physiology
KN 441.description: Examination of skeletal muscle function during physical activity and adaptations of skeletal muscle that occur with exercise training, inactivity and aging. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 352.

KN 442.name: Principles of ECG Interpretation
KN 442.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KN 352; and junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture/Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 442.description: Introduction to the basic principles and interpretation of the electrocardiogram (ECG) as it relates to fitness programs involving the apparently healthy as well as cardiac rehabilitation patients.  Course Information:  Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KN 352; and junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture/Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 445.name: Corrective Exercise for Postural and Movement Imbalances
KN 445.prerequisites: KN 243. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.
KN 445.description: Focuses on how to develop, integrate, and apply comprehensive strategies to identify, assess, and improve common postural and muscular imbalances which contributes to movement impairments. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 243. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Laboratory and one Lecture-Discussion.

KN 447.name: Mental Health in Athletics
KN 447.description: Designed to advance student understanding and response to current and emerging mental health issues in athletics. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above.

KN 448.name: Adapted Exercise Programming
KN 448.description: Examines the criteria for exercise and fitness participation for individuals with disabilities or chronic health conditions. Course Information: Previously listed as KN 348. Prerequisite(s): KN 345.

KN 450.name: Exercise Programming for Athletic Performance
KN 450.description: Students develop the required knowledge and competencies to complete professional credential examinations with nationally and internationally recognized organizations such as the National Strength and Conditioning Association. Course Information: KN 345 or consent of instructor.

KN 452.name: Advanced Exercise Physiology
KN 452.prerequisites: KN 352. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.
KN 452.description: Builds on the science foundation provided by KN 352 to examine timely and emerging topics in exercise physiology. Students will develop skills for critical thinking, problem solving, and forming and defending a scientific opinion. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 352. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Laboratory.

KN 460.name: Neuromechanical Basis of Human Movement
KN 460.description: Biomechanics of single and multi-joint systems, and its role in neural control of movement.  Mechanisms of acute adaptations including warm-up, fatigue and potentiation, and chronic adaptations arising from reduced use or training.  Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 361.

KN 465.name: Biomechanics of the Neuromusculoskeletal Systems
KN 465.prerequisites: KN 361 or one year of college physics; or consent of the instructor.
KN 465.description: Introduces the non-engineering/physics student to the biomechanics of the neural, muscular and skeletal systems. The course focuses on normal structure-function of tissues and joints, injury and prevention. Course Information: Previously listed as KN 365. Prerequisite(s): KN 361 or one year of college physics; or consent of the instructor.

KN 472.name: Movement Neuroscience
KN 472.prerequisites: KN 252; or KN 231 and KN 233. Recommended background: KN 352 and KN 372.
KN 472.description: Overview of the neurophysiology of human movement.  This course will cover the mechanisms and neural circuitry of sensory and sensorimotor processes underlying the control of movement. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): KN 252; or KN 231 and KN 233. Recommended background: KN 352 and KN 372.

KN 475.name: Movement Disorders
KN 475.description: Examines basic and applied understanding of the neural changes in motor function in disease and disorders of movement. This will include peripheral and central motor deficits. Prerequisite(s): KN 352 and KN 372.

KN 491.name: Professional Preparation
KN 491.prerequisites: Junior standing or above. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion.
KN 491.description: Advances student employability and graduate school success in five domains: problem solving, communication, team work, adaptability, and emotional intelligence; helps students develop five key skill competencies. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion.

KN 493.name: Practicum in Undergraduate Teaching
KN 493.prerequisites: Students must have successfully completed the course in which they will be assisting (or an equivalent) and obtain consent of the instructor.
KN 493.description: Peer instruction experience for undergraduate students. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Students may register for more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Students must have successfully completed the course in which they will be assisting (or an equivalent) and obtain consent of the instructor.

KN 494.name: Special Topics in Kinesiology
KN 494.prerequisites: Depending on topic, specific prerequisites may be required.
KN 494.description: Flexible course structure designed to accommodate relevant topics beyond the scope of the current course offerings, with more in-depth analysis of primary literature. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Depending on topic, specific prerequisites may be required.

KN 495.name: Senior Seminar
KN 495.description: Challenges students to integrate concepts learned in previous courses to solve problems and come to conclusions on health-related topics. It also prepares students for their immediate post-baccalaureate professional steps. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Senior standing or above.

KN 496.name: Special Projects in Kinesiology
KN 496.prerequisites: Approval by graduate faculty member and graduate director.
KN 496.description: Independent research on special projects. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Approval by graduate faculty member and graduate director.

KOR 101.name: Elementary Korean I
KOR 101.description: Introduction to Korean language and culture. Intensive practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Course Information: Course is taught in a blended-online format. Extensive computer use is required. A high-speed connection is strongly suggested. Seniors interested in this course must contact the Korean BLP Coordinator.

KOR 102.name: Elementary Korean II
KOR 102.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KOR 101; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
KOR 102.description: A continuation of KOR 101. An elementary level examination of Korean language and culture. Intensive practice in speaking, listening, reading and writing. Course Information: This course is taught in a blended-online format. Extensive computer use is required. A high-speed connection is strongly suggested. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KOR 101; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

KOR 103.name: Intermediate Korean I
KOR 103.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KOR 102; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
KOR 103.description: A continuation of KOR 102. An intermediate-level examination of Korean language and culture. Intensive practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Course Information: This course is taught in a blended-online format. Extensive computer use is required. A high-speed connection is strongly suggested. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KOR 102; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

KOR 104.name: Intermediate Korean II
KOR 104.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KOR 103; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
KOR 104.description: This fourth of a four-semester basic language program in Korean and continuation of KOR 103. An intermediate level examination of Korean language and culture. Intensive practice in speaking, listening, reading, and writing. Course Information: This course is taught in a blended-online format. Extensive computer use is required. A high-speed connection is strongly suggested. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KOR 103; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

KOR 130.name: Understanding Korean History, Culture and Society through Contemporary Korean Films
KOR 130.description: Exploration of contemporary Korean history and culture through film. Course Information: Same as MOVI 130. Taught in English. UIC GE World Cultures course.

KOR 201.name: Advanced Korean I
KOR 201.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in KOR 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
KOR 201.description: The study of Korean at a low-advanced level. Students will be involved with intensive listening, speaking, reading and writing used in low-advanced formal and informal communicative situations. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in KOR 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

KOR 230.name: Korean Popular Culture in a Global World
KOR 230.description: An introduction to the global phenomenon of Korean popular culture, including music, film, and television. Examines diverse K-Pop themes such as cross-cultural impacts and its possibilities for the future. Course Information: Taught in English. UIC GE World Cultures course.

LAT 101.name: Elementary Latin I
LAT 101.description: Fundamentals of the Latin language. Grammar and reading. Course Information: For students who have no credit in Latin. One additional hour of computer-assisted instruction each week.

LAT 102.name: Elementary Latin II
LAT 102.description: Continues LAT 101. Grammar and reading. Course Information: One additional hour of computer-assisted instruction each week. Prerequisite(s): LAT 101 or the equivalent.

LAT 103.name: Intermediate Latin I
LAT 103.description: Introduction to Roman historians and oratory. Selections from Cicero, Sallust, Livy, and other Latin prose writers. Review of forms and grammar. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): LAT 102 or the equivalent.

LAT 104.name: Intermediate Latin II
LAT 104.prerequisites: LAT 103 or the equivalent. IAI GECC Humanities course.
LAT 104.description: Completes study of Latin at intermediate level. Latin poetry as well as prose, and grammar. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): LAT 103 or the equivalent. IAI GECC Humanities course.

LAT 291.name: Topics in Latin Prose Authors
LAT 291.prerequisites: LAT 104 or equivalent for students who register in LAT 291 only. Recommended background: CL 102. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.
LAT 291.description: Readings in Latin prose chosen from a single author, period, or single genre. Course Information: Same as CL 291. May be repeated for a maximum of 9 hours if topics vary. Taught in English. Students who register in CL 291 will complete assignments in English translation. Students who register in LAT 291 will complete more than half of the readings in Latin. Prerequisite(s): LAT 104 or equivalent for students who register in LAT 291 only. Recommended background: CL 102. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.

LAT 299.name: Independent Reading
LAT 299.prerequisites: LAT 104 or the equivalent. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.
LAT 299.description: Individual study under faculty direction. For students qualified by preparation and interest. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): LAT 104 or the equivalent. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.

LAT 391.name: Topics in Latin Poetry
LAT 391.prerequisites: CL 250 or CL 252 or CL 253; or consent of the instructor; and LAT 291 or the equivalent for students who register for LAT 391 only. Recommended background: CL 102. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.
LAT 391.description: Advanced readings in Latin poetry chosen from a single author, period, or single genre in different periods. Course Information: Same as CL 391. May be repeated to a maximum of 9 hours if topics vary. Taught in English. Students who register in CL 391 will complete assignments in English translation. Students who register in LAT 391 will complete more than half of the readings in Latin. Prerequisite(s): CL 250 or CL 252 or CL 253; or consent of the instructor; and LAT 291 or the equivalent for students who register for LAT 391 only. Recommended background: CL 102. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.

LALS 100.name: Skills and Career Readiness in Latin American and Latino Studies
LALS 100.description: Success skills and career readiness for the major or minor, developing a career plan with an LALS degree, identifying and taking advantage of college resources, and learning to meet university expectations. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading. LALS 100 does not count toward the LALS major or minor.

LALS 101.name: Introduction to Latin American Studies
LALS 101.description: Introduction to the major concepts, issues, and debates in the field of Latin American Studies. Overview of history, cultures, and issues of race, ethnicity, gender, class in Latin America.  Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 102.name: Introduction to Latino Studies
LALS 102.description: Introduction to the major concepts, issues, and debates in the field of U.S. Latina/o Studies. Overview of the history, cultures, and issues of race, ethnicity, gender, and class among Latinos in the United States.  Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. IAI GECC Fine Arts course, and IAI GECC Humanities course, and UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 103.name: Introduction to Latino Urban Studies
LALS 103.description: Demographic, economic, political, cultural, and social dimensions of Latino communities in the United States. Includes Chicanos/Mexicanos Puerto Ricans, Cubans, and Central and South Americans. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 104.name: Introduction to Puerto Rican Studies
LALS 104.description: Analysis of historical, cultural, political, social and economic issues of Puerto Rico: the political status of Puerto Rico, problems of cultural identity, migration and economic dependence. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 105.name: Introduction to Mexican Studies
LALS 105.description: Introduction to major issues in the formation of modern Mexico (conquest, Revolution of 1910) and to major historical and contemporary literary works which depict and interpret the Mexican and Mexico. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 107.name: Introduction to Nahua Studies
LALS 107.description: Survey of key aspects of Aztec (Nahua) society, culture, and language in the era before and during European colonization (1300 CE-present). Introduces basic reading skills in Nahuatl, a major indigenous language in Central Mexico. UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 108.name: Indigenous Culture Change in Latin America
LALS 108.description: Overview of Latin American indigenous societies from the Precolumbian era to the present, using archaeological, historical, and anthropological findings to analyze the changes and adaptations of native cultures from Latin America. UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 109.name: Introduction to Latino Cultures
LALS 109.description: Examination of the cultural and artistic productions of U.S. Latinos and/or Latin Americans through historical processes of mainstreaming, transculturation, and hybridity. Course Information: Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 110.name: Introduction to Latin American Cultures
LALS 110.description: Examination of the cultural and artistic productions of Latin Americans and constructions of Latin American heritage and tradition. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecure and one Discussion. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 120.name: Introduction to Research in Latin American and Latino Studies
LALS 120.description: Immersion experience with research in the Latino community. A guided exploration of the research process, including production of a research question, choosing methods for collecting data, and producing a poster presentation to share findings. Course Information: Field work required.

LALS 127.name: Latin American Music
LALS 127.description: Survey class that introduces students to the rich repertoire of music in Latin America. It explores the history of genres, their development, instruments and representative artists in their geographical, social and cultural contexts.  Course Information:  Same as MUS 127. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 161.name: Introduction to Latin American History
LALS 161.description: Introduction to major themes in Latin American history from pre-Colombian society and the European conquest to the present. Course Information: Same as HIST 161. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 192.name: Latin American Women Writers in Translation
LALS 192.description: Introduction to literature by Latin American women from the seventeenth century to the present. Focus on the role literature has played in the negotiation of gender identities in the private and the public spheres. Course Information: Same as GWS 192 and SPAN 192. Taught in English. Extensive computer use required. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 217.name: Human Geography of Latin America including the Caribbean Region
LALS 217.description: Culture, settlement, political and economic development problems in Latin America, with special attention to Puerto Rico, the Caribbean Region, and Mexico. Course Information: Same as GEOG 203. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 220.name: Latin American Cities in a Global Context: Environment, Employment, and Housing
LALS 220.description: Global economic trends impacting Latin American cities, with a focus on three urban planning issues: environment, employment and housing.  Growing inequality, precarious employment, spatial segregation, and squatter settlements will be explored. Course Information: Same as US 220. Recommended background: LALS 101. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 223.name: Southwestern Archaeology
LALS 223.prerequisites: Credit or concurrent registration in ANTH 102; or consent of the instructor.
LALS 223.description: Introduction to the archaeology of ancient Native American cultures of the North American Southwest, emphasizing processes of culture change from earliest times until European contact, with focus on current debates and relevant methods and theories. Course Information: Same as ANTH 223. Prerequisite(s): Credit or concurrent registration in ANTH 102; or consent of the instructor.

LALS 225.name: Racial and Ethnic Groups
LALS 225.prerequisites: SOC 100; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.
LALS 225.description: Sociological and social-psychological analysis of racial, religious, and other ethnic groups; consideration of historical and current social problems arising from their relationships in society. Course Information: Same as BLST 225 and SOC 225. Prerequisite(s): SOC 100; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 229.name: Sociology of Latinos
LALS 229.prerequisites: SOC 100 or LALS 102 or SOC 105 or LALS 103; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.
LALS 229.description: Examines social, political and economic issues affecting Latino groups.   While focusing on the process of racialization, the course also examines immigration, language rights, gender and sexuality, labor market, media, and youth. Course Information: Same as SOC 229. Prerequisite(s): SOC 100 or LALS 102 or SOC 105 or LALS 103; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 233.name: Latinos in Chicago
LALS 233.description: Development and dynamics of Chicago's Mexican, Puerto Rican, Cuban, Central and South American communities: settlement, demographics, economics, culture, social institutions and political participation.

LALS 234.name: Politics of Immigration Enforcement
LALS 234.description: How immigrants in the United States have been constructed as a “problem” and ways that authorities have consequently sought to govern them. Topics include nativism, illegalization, criminalization, border policing, detention, deportation, and rights. Course Information: Same as ANTH 246.

LALS 239.name: Visual Culture of the Ancient Andes
LALS 239.description: A survey of the visual expressions, material culture, and built environment of ancient Andean civilization, from the earliest manifestations of societal complexity through the Spanish Conquest, with particular attention to the environmental, ideologi. Course Information: Same as AH 273. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 240.name: Visual Culture of Ancient Mesoamerica
LALS 240.description: A survey of the visual expressions, material culture, and built environment of ancient Mesoamerican civilization, from the earliest manifestations of societal complexity through the Spanish Conquest, with particular attention to the environmental, id. Course Information: Same as AH 274. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 242.name: Government and Politics of Latin America
LALS 242.description: An examination of government and politics in selected Latin American countries. Comparative and historical analysis of dictatorship, democracies, political institutions, and parties. Course Information: Same as POLS 242.

LALS 257.name: Archaeology of North America
LALS 257.prerequisites: ANTH 102 or consent of the instructor.
LALS 257.description: Introduction to the prehistoric cultures of North America from earliest times until the arrival of Europeans. Course Information: Same as ANTH 226. Prerequisite(s): ANTH 102 or consent of the instructor.

LALS 258.name: Ancient Civilizations of Mexico and Central America
LALS 258.prerequisites: ANTH 102; or sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor.
LALS 258.description: Analysis and interpretation of the archaeological evidence on the process of development of native civilization in the Meso-American area from the beginnings of agricultural settlement to the eve of the Spanish conquest. Course Information: Same as ANTH 227 and GEOG 207. Prerequisite(s): ANTH 102; or sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor.

LALS 260.name: Indigenous Storytelling in Latin America
LALS 260.description: Study of Legend of the Suns, the Quetzalcoatl cycle, pictographic stories, the Popol Vuh and tales from Huarochirí in the colonial context. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 261.name: Latin America to 1850
LALS 261.prerequisites: ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor.Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
LALS 261.description: Provides a survey of the pre-Columbian and early national periods in Latin America. Course Information: Same as HIST 261. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor.Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 262.name: Latin America Since 1850
LALS 262.prerequisites: ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
LALS 262.description: Provides an introduction to Latin American socioeconomic, political, and cultural development since 1850 with emphasis on major countries and regions. Course Information: Same as HIST 262. Course is offered in both face-to-face and online formats. Check the class schedule for details on specific sections. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 263.name: Latin American Colonial Art
LALS 263.prerequisites: Three hours of art history at the 100 level, or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
LALS 263.description: A survey of Latin American art and architecture from European contact to independence. Course Information:  Same as AH 263.  Prerequisite(s): Three hours of art history at the 100 level, or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 266.name: Modern Mexico
LALS 266.prerequisites: ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor.Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
LALS 266.description: Provides an introduction into the social, cultural, and political history of Mexico from the decades after independence to the present. Course Information: Same as HIST 266. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161; or consent of the instructor.Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 267.name: Introduction to U
LALS 267.description: Introductory survey of a wide range of U.S. Latinx literature in various genres and various ethnic, geographical and historical contexts. Course Information: Same as ENGL 267. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

LALS 268.name: History of Central America
LALS 268.prerequisites: Completion of the English composition requirement. UIC GE Past course.
LALS 268.description: The human and territorial characteristics of Central America, including facets of Spanish colonialism and the history of revolutions and modernity in the region, including a history of U.S. - Central American relations. Course Information: Same as HIST 269. Prerequisite(s): Completion of the English composition requirement. UIC GE Past course.

LALS 269.name: Latin American Environmental Studies
LALS 269.description: Overview of environmental challenges in Latin America caused by mining, export agriculture, petroleum exploration, urbanization, and climate change. Social and political responses to environmental change and conservation regulation. Course Information: Recommended background: EAES 101. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 270.name: Ethnography of Meso-America
LALS 270.prerequisites: ENGL 161. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
LALS 270.description: Survey of the contemporary indigenous cultures of Meso-America, studied against their pre-conquest history and in their development since the Spanish Conquest. Course Information: Same as ANTH 277. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 274.name: The Politics of Blackness in Latin America
LALS 274.description: Examines race, culture, and politics in Latin America from historical and contemporary perspectives with a particular emphasis on the latter half of the 20th century and the beginning of the 21st century. Course Information: Same as BLST 274 and POLS 274. Recommended background: Three credit hours in BLST or LALS or POLS.

LALS 275.name: Gender in Latin America
LALS 275.description: Latin American women in historical perspective from pre-Columbian and Iberian societies to the present. Course Information: Same as GWS 275 and POLS 275. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion/Recitation. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 276.name: Latinas in the United States
LALS 276.description: Socioeconomic conditions and cultural experiences of Latinas in the U.S. Historical and contemporary views of labor, health, education, family, identity formation and leadership. Course Information: Same as GWS 276, and SOC 226. Grade of C or better in ENGL 160; Grade of C or better or concurrent registration in ENGL 161. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 277.name: Issues of Race, Class, and Gender Among Latinos
LALS 277.description: Institutional, cultural and psychological components of race, class, and gender relations. Institutional inequality, questions of assimilation and identity, attitudes, and effects of inequality on community.

LALS 278.name: Latin American/Latino Film Studies
LALS 278.description: Introduction to the history and major aesthetic movements of Latin American cinema. Course Information: Same as MOVI 278 and SPAN 278. Taught in English. Includes a film screening component. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

LALS 283.name: Latinos and Politics
LALS 283.description: Latino politics and politicians in the context of the American political system. The political system, Latino participation, experience, and research on political processes. Course Information: Same as POLS 209. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 286.name: Issues in Latino Identity
LALS 286.description: Examines one or more topics of central importance to U.S. Latino populations. Topics may be related to such issues as youth, migration, family, religion, or cultural production.

LALS 288.name: History of Modern Puerto Rico
LALS 288.description: Survey of political and socioeconomic history from 1868 to the present. Course Information: Same as HIST 288.

LALS 289.name: Latina/o History
LALS 289.prerequisites: ENGL 161. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course.
LALS 289.description: Latinas/os in the United States from the colonial era to the present. Empire and imperialism, migration and citizenship, diversity and community formation, politics, labor, activism, and transnational ties to Latin America. Same as HIST 289. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course.

LALS 290.name: Mexican-American History
LALS 290.description: The political, social, economic, and cultural development of the Mexican people in the U.S. from colonial times until the present. Course Information: Same as HIST 290. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 291.name: Topics in Latin America
LALS 291.description: In-depth focus on a particular theme or topic relating to Latin American society, politics, culture, or economics. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Recommended background: LALS 101.

LALS 292.name: Topics in Latino Studies
LALS 292.description: In-depth focus on a particular theme or topic relating to Latinos in the US or transnational issues relating to society, politics, culture, or economics. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term.

LALS 295.name: Latino Literary Studies
LALS 295.description: Major trends, genres, works, themes, and writers related to Latino history and culture, mainstream and minority U.S., Latin American and third world literatures. Course Information: Same as ENGL 295 and SPAN 295. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE US Society course.

LALS 296.name: Latin American and Latino Studies Study Abroad
LALS 296.prerequisites: Acceptance to the program via the Study Abroad Office application process and consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice.
LALS 296.description: International field work exploring cultural, historical, and sociopolitical issues of different communities within Latin American and Latino Studies. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Taught in English. Field trips required at a nominal fee. Field work required. Students must apply and be accepted by the UIC Study Abroad Office in order to register for this course. Visit www.studyabroad.uic.edu for more information. Prerequisite(s): Acceptance to the program via the Study Abroad Office application process and consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice.

LALS 299.name: Independent Study
LALS 299.prerequisites: A 2.50 grade point average. Open to undergraduate students with consent of the appropriate instructor and the Latin American and Latino Studies director. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.
LALS 299.description: Individual reading or research project and paper in Latin American or U.S. Latino Studies, with instructor's consent and supervision. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 9 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): A 2.50 grade point average. Open to undergraduate students with consent of the appropriate instructor and the Latin American and Latino Studies director. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.

LALS 301.name: Research Methods in Latin America and Latino Studies
LALS 301.prerequisites: Two 200-level LALS courses; LALS major or minor or consent of the instructor; junior standing or above or consent of the instructor.
LALS 301.description: An examination of various research methods used in Latin America and Latino Studies. Qualitative research methods used in the humanities and social sciences with emphasis on how to formulate ideas, develop them, and carry out a research project.  Course Information:  Prerequisite(s):  Two 200-level LALS courses; LALS major or minor or consent of the instructor; junior standing or above or consent of the instructor.

LALS 302.name: Research Workshop in Latin American and Latino Studies
LALS 302.prerequisites: LALS 301, junior standing or above, and consent of the instructor.
LALS 302.description: Workshop where students will engage in individual research projects related to Latin American and/or Latino Studies.  Course Information:  Prerequisite(s):  LALS 301, junior standing or above, and consent of the instructor.

LALS 304.name: Introduction to Spanish Creative Writing for Heritage Speakers
LALS 304.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in SPAN 204; or consent of the instructor. Recommended background: Grade of C or better in SPAN 210.
LALS 304.description: Exploration of writing tools through analysis of Spanish literary texts and through creative writing exercises. Course Information: Same as SPAN 304. This course may not be used to satisfy the prerequisite for ENGL 490, ENGL 491, or ENGL 492. Extensive computer use required. This course is intended for heritage students, but may be taken by students demonstrating near-native writing proficiency with the permission of the course instructor. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in SPAN 204; or consent of the instructor. Recommended background: Grade of C or better in SPAN 210.

LALS 328.name: Child Migration Across the Americas
LALS 328.description: Examines why and how migrant children have played a central role in the past and present migratory dynamics of the Americas. Course Information: Same as ANTH 328.

LALS 333.name: Undocumented Social Movements
LALS 333.description: Undocumented coalitions, visibility, and refusal. Poetry, films, artivism, movement journalism, and secondary readings. Visibility, claiming and redefining citizenship, and building power alongside other racialized communities. Course Information: Same as ANTH 333.

LALS 335.name: Latinx Sound Studies
LALS 335.description: Experience and experiment with sound to imagine and enact social identities and leverage political power. Intersections of race, class, gender and sexuality, geography and citizenship through sounded and aural frameworks. Course Information: Same as COMM 334.

LALS 337.name: Latino/a Religions in the US
LALS 337.description: How Latinx communities venerate ancestors and deities, create and remake sacred space, practice healing, and organize politically through the lens of of documentaries, fiction and literature, visual art, and ethnographic studies. Course Information: Same as  RELS 337.

LALS 348.name: Seminar: Political Problems of Developing Societies
LALS 348.prerequisites: POLS 200 and POLS 130; or consent of the instructor.
LALS 348.description: Selected aspects of the politics and countries of Asia, Africa and Latin America. Course Information: Same as POLS 348. Prerequisite(s): POLS 200 and POLS 130; or consent of the instructor.

LALS 367.name: Latinx Literature
LALS 367.description: Development of Latinx literature from different historical periods and diasporic communities. Analysis of political and social concerns relevant to Latinx literature and culture. Course Information: Same as ENGL 367. Previously listed as LALS 353. Recommended background: Any of LALS 101-102 or ENGL 207, 209, or 267.

LALS 376.name: Politics of the Latinx Body
LALS 376.description: Focuses on the social construction of raced, gendered, and sexed bodies as deviant, abnormal, and pathological, and on how such bodies are rendered objects of surveillance, discipline, and regulation. Particular attention is paid to Latinx bodies.

LALS 380.name: Social Movements in Latin America
LALS 380.prerequisites: One course in LALS or consent of the instructor.
LALS 380.description: The different ways in which different groups have used non-traditional means to change the social and political circumstances that have conditioned their lives. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): One course in LALS or consent of the instructor.

LALS 385.name: Latino Social Movements in the United States
LALS 385.prerequisites: One course in LALS or consent of the instructor.
LALS 385.description: Activism and social movements of Latina/o populations in the United States. Topics may include labor organizing, civil rights and power movements, immigrant mobilizations, feminism, and environmental justice. Course Information: Prerequisite(s):  One course in LALS or consent of the instructor.

LALS 391.name: Seminar in Latin American Studies
LALS 391.description: Diverse aspects of modern Latin American society, politics, culture and economics from the wars of independence to contemporary times.  Course Information:  May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Recommended background: LALS 101.

LALS 395.name: Seminar in Latino Studies
LALS 395.description: Diverse aspects of the U.S. Latino experience at more theoretical and advanced levels. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Recommended background: LALS 102.

LALS 403.name: Interdisciplinary Research Methods in Latin American and Latino Studies
LALS 403.prerequisites: Graduate standing. Undergraduates may register with consent of the instructor. Recommended background: Credit or concurrent registration in LALS 501.
LALS 403.description: Examination of research methods in social sciences and current trends in Latin American and Latino Studies. Emphasis on critical analysis of research methods, use of analytical approaches for particular kinds of investigation, and hands on application to case studies. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing. Undergraduates may register with consent of the instructor. Recommended background: Credit or concurrent registration in LALS 501.

LALS 408.name: Global Black Feminisms
LALS 408.description: Examines transnational, Third Wave and Third World Black feminist approaches to global politics from historical and contemporary perspectives with a particular emphasis on the late 20th century and the beginning of the 21st century. Course Information: Same as BLST 408 and GWS 408. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours.  Recommended background: Undergraduate students: at least three credit hours in BLST or GWS or LALS.

LALS 417.name: Latina and Latin American Feminist Thought
LALS 417.description: Chicana/Latina and Latin American feminist texts and methods. Differential consciousness, spirituality (spiritual activism and spiritual mestizaje), curanderismo, and decolonial epistemologies. Course Information: Same as GWS 417.

LALS 422.name: Chicanas and Latinas: Self and Society
LALS 422.description: The experiences of Chicanas and Latinas through the lens of contemporary sociological research. Topics to be discussed include: community formation and activism, feminisms, sexuality, religion, health, family, immigration, education, work, media, etc. Course Information: Same as SOC 422.

LALS 423.name: Andean Prehistory
LALS 423.prerequisites: ANTH 269 or consent of the instructor.
LALS 423.description: An overview of the cultural evolution of the Andean region from the arrival of the first inhabitants to the development of the Inca empire.  Course Information:  Same as ANTH 423.  3 undergraduate hours.  4 graduate hours.  Prerequisite(s): ANTH 269 or consent of the instructor.

LALS 425.name: Urban Field Research in Latino/a Communities
LALS 425.description: Field work in Chicago Latino/a neighborhoods. Observing, taking field notes, analyzing issues of workforce, homelessness, street vending, affordable housing, community organizing, educational infrastructure, environmental degradation, gentrification. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Field work required. Prerequisite(s): LALS 403.

LALS 428.name: Latin@ Futurity
LALS 428.description: An exploration of utopic and dystopic imaginations in Latin@ literature, media, and popular culture. Discusses coloniality, precarity, an alternate timeline as (re)imagined through queer, indigenous, and femme epistemologies. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Same as COMM 428.

LALS 433.name: Latin American Migration to the U
LALS 433.prerequisites: One course in LALS or SOC; or consent of the instructor.
LALS 433.description: Latin American migration to the U.S. International migration theories, family remittances, transnational linkages, dual citizenship, and past and current U.S. immigration policy debates. Course Information: Same as SOC 433. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): One course in LALS or SOC; or consent of the instructor.

LALS 434.name: Literature of the Americas
LALS 434.description: Transnational or hemispheric approach to Latin American literature. Writers whose work moves back and forth between nations. Identities across borders, languages, and nations. Course Information: Same as ENGL 434. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours.

LALS 436.name: Borders and Borderlands: Method and Theory
LALS 436.description: Borders as theory and method in Latino/a and Latin American Studies. Borders and bordering practices, mobile, transnational communities, mapping and policing, and migrant mobility and autonomy. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours.

LALS 437.name: Global Migrations Across the Americas
LALS 437.description: Processes of migrant mobilities and control in the Americas. Structural causes, social groups, and role of global inequality. Case studies in the US and Latin America regarding the securitization of migration and growth patterns in migrant mobility. Course Information: Same as ANTH 435. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours.

LALS 461.name: Topics in Latin American History
LALS 461.prerequisites: 3 hours of history, Latin American and Latino studies, or consent of the instructor.
LALS 461.description: Specific topics are announced each term. Course Information: Same as HIST 461. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): 3 hours of history, Latin American and Latino studies, or consent of the instructor.

LALS 465.name: Asian Diasporas in Latin America
LALS 465.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in GLAS 100 or Grade of C or better in HIST 161. Recommended background: HIST 264.
LALS 465.description: Examines Asian migration to Latin America, covering topics such as labor and economic systems, transnational networks, community building, racism, identity, and serial migration. Course Information: Same as GLAS 465 and HIST 465. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in GLAS 100 or Grade of C or better in HIST 161. Recommended background: HIST 264.
