Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours.  4 graduate hours. Previously listed as PA 463. Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PA 432.name: Management of Information Technology
PA 432.prerequisites: Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.
PA 432.description: The management of technology in government and nonprofit organizations, including typical organizational models; technology roles, skills, and leadership; integration of technology with business operations; and common technology challenges. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as PA 461. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PA 433.name: Data Management
PA 433.prerequisites: Graduate or professional standing; or admission to the MPA program or consent of the instructor.
PA 433.description: An overview of the use and leveraging of data in order to allow government and nonprofit organizations to make better decisions and improve operations. Topics to be covered will include the concepts of data management and governance. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as PA 460. Prerequisite(s): Graduate or professional standing; or admission to the MPA program or consent of the instructor.

PA 434.name: Intermediate Data Management and Analysis
PA 434.prerequisites: PA 402 and Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.
PA 434.description: Latest practices for gaining better value from data, emphasizing hands-on use and application of data analytics for univariate and bivariate statistics and data visualization. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Extensive computer use required. Prerequisite(s): PA 402 and Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PA 435.name: Geographic Information Systems (GIS) for Public Managers
PA 435.prerequisites: Latest practices for gaining better value from data, emphasizing hands-on use and application of data analytics for univariate and bivariate statistics and data visualization.
PA 435.description: Fundamental GIS tools and applications as well as the challenges in implementing and sustaining a GIS function in the public setting. Course information: Prerequisite(s): Latest practices for gaining better value from data, emphasizing hands-on use and application of data analytics for univariate and bivariate statistics and data visualization.

PA 446.name: Coding for Civic Data Applications
PA 446.prerequisites: PA 402; or PA 434; or consent of the instructor and admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Computer Applications program. Recommended background: Familiarity with coding environments and programming. To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Laboratory.
PA 446.description: Advanced tech skills for coding, transforming, and visualizing data using existing platforms, with an emphasis on R. Topics include functional programming, GitHub and webpage creation, web scraping, Shiny applications, and advanced data visualization. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): PA 402; or PA 434; or consent of the instructor and admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Computer Applications program. Recommended background: Familiarity with coding environments and programming. To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Laboratory.

PA 447.name: Survey Planning and Design
PA 447.prerequisites: Graduate or professional standing and BSTT 400 and CHSC 446; or approval of the department.Recommended background:  Credit in CHSC 446 or the equivalent.
PA 447.description: Theory and applications of sample survey planning and design for conducting research in health sciences and related fields. Addresses three major topics: survey design and planning, sampling, and data collection procedures. Course Information:   Same as CHSC 447.  Prerequisite(s):  Graduate or professional standing and BSTT 400 and CHSC 446; or approval of the department.Recommended background:  Credit in CHSC 446 or the equivalent.

PA 452.name: The UIC Freshwater Lab Course
PA 452.description: Brings a Humanities lens to the Great Lakes watershed to examine how water interacts with socio-political systems, legal structures, cultural perceptions, and artistic visions. Course Information: Same as ENGL 452 and UPP 452. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 160 and ENGL 161.

PA 453.name: Freshwater Lab in Practice: Environmental Research, Projects, and Internships
PA 453.description: Investigates key issues surrounding Great Lakes water governance through a combination of hands-on research and professional development in fields related to environmental policy, planning, writing and communication. Course Information: Same as ENGL 453 and UPP 453. 3 undergraduate hours; 4 graduate hours. This course offers an internship placement opportunity. Depending on placement/start date, students may begin internship work during the second half of the semester or during the summer. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 160 and ENGL 161.

PA 464.name: Technology and Innovation Theory
PA 464.prerequisites: Graduate or professional standing and admission to the Ph.D. in Public Administration program or consent of the instructor.
PA 464.description: The course focuses on theories surrounding the creation, development, transfer, and use of technology. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Meets eight weeks of the semester. Prerequisite(s): Graduate or professional standing and admission to the Ph.D. in Public Administration program or consent of the instructor.

PA 466.name: Science, Technology and Public Policy
PA 466.prerequisites: Graduate standing; or consent of the instructor and admission to the Ph.D. in Public Administration program or consent of the instructor.
PA 466.description: Addresses the relationships between public policy and science and technology in the United States. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing; or consent of the instructor and admission to the Ph.D. in Public Administration program or consent of the instructor.

PA 467.name: Civic Engagement
PA 467.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program; or consent of instructor.
PA 467.description: Analytic frameworks and practical strategies for utilizing public participation in government and community decisions. Investigates interactions between civic participation, efforts to improve administration of programs and develop new public policies. Course Information: Same as PPOL 467. 3 undergraduate hours; 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as PA 564. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program; or consent of instructor.

PA 468.name: Topics in Management and Leadership of Public Organizations
PA 468.prerequisites: Admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.
PA 468.description: Examines management and leadership issues through experiences of current and former public managers, political appointees, elected officials, and other key public leaders. Emphasis on topics from Illinois and Chicago metropolitan region. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PA 470.name: AI & Machine Learning
PA 470.prerequisites: PA 402 or PA 434; and admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.
PA 470.description: Introduces students to public sector applications of artificial intelligence and machine learning. It begins with the philosophical foundations of artificial intelligence before introducing students to major frameworks for learning. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): PA 402 or PA 434; and admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PA 490.name: Field Experience in Public Policy and Administration
PA 490.prerequisites: Admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics, Bachelor of Arts in Public Policy, or Bachelor of Arts in Urban Studies program; or consent of the instructor.
PA 490.description: Students work in an organization such as a government agency, community group, or nonprofit organization. Students are required to submit written work and meet with professor on periodic basis to review work experience. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. May be repeated. A maximum of 4 hours of credit may be applied to the Master of Public Administration program. Field work required. Students who have no prior work experience in the public or non-profit sectors are strongly recommended to register for this course. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Master of Public Administration or Master of Public Policy or Master of Science in Civic Analytics, Bachelor of Arts in Public Policy, or Bachelor of Arts in Urban Studies program; or consent of the instructor.

PA 494.name: Special Topics in Public Administration
PA 494.prerequisites: Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.
PA 494.description: Consideration of timely or enduring issues in public administration not available in regularly offered courses. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated to a maximum of 12 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Master of Public Administration, Master of Public Policy, or Master of Science in Civic Analytics program or consent of the instructor.

PUBH 100.name: Health and the Public
PUBH 100.description: Students will examine both historical and contemporary public health stories focusing on the United States to begin to understand the contexts, systems, professions, tools, and skills associated with the public health enterprise. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

PUBH 105.name: Foundations for Public Health
PUBH 105.description: Engages pre-public health students by deepening their knowledge and understanding of careers in the public health arena. Students will develop and refine foundational skills that support successful matriculation into the public health major. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. Field trips required at a nominal fee. Enrollment will be restricted to first year students in the Public Health - Undecided (Pre-Public Health) program and other Pre-Public Health students by permission of the instructor.

PUBH 110.name: Public Health and Global Societies
PUBH 110.description: Introduces students to global public health through an exploration of health challenges from the local population to global society perspectives. Course Information: Field trip required at a nominal fee. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

PUBH 120.name: Public Health and the Study of Disease and Epidemics
PUBH 120.description: Introduces basic principles surrounding the distribution of disease and epidemics in human populations. Through lectures and field exercises, students will learn fundamentals of epidemiology, the basic science of public health. UIC GE Natural World No Lab course.

PUBH 130.name: Introductory Public Health Statistics for Public Health Decision-Making
PUBH 130.prerequisites: Satisfactory grade in MATH 090, or passing grade in MATH 108, or appropriate score on the department placement test. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Natural World No Lab course.
PUBH 130.description: An introductory statistics course through the lens of medical, biological, and public health disciplines. Topics covered include study design, sampling, descriptive statistics and basic hypothesis tests for comparing groups. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Prerequisite(s): Satisfactory grade in MATH 090, or passing grade in MATH 108, or appropriate score on the department placement test. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Natural World No Lab course.

PUBH 194.name: Introductory Special Topics in Public Health
PUBH 194.description: Introductory analysis of selected public health challenges and issues. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term.

PUBH 200.name: Exploring Connections between Human Health and the Environment
PUBH 200.description:
Exploration of case studies related to human health and the environment, with engagement from community partners. Students evaluate environmental health issues, human health of workers, and use data to plan advocacy activities. Course Information: Recommended background:
PUBH 100 or other course(s) related to Understanding the Individual and Society. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

PUBH 205.name: Public Health Seminar II
PUBH 205.description: Intended for second year students in the Public Health major, will support students’ growth in preparation for work in the public health profession with a focus on applied activities and exercises. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 105.

PUBH 299.name: Undergraduate Public Health Study Abroad
PUBH 299.prerequisites: Consent of the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health Program and the Study Abroad Office.
PUBH 299.description: Provides credit for foreign study. Student's proposal for study abroad course work must have prior approval of the BA in Public Health Program. Final determination of credit is made upon the student's completion of the study abroad course work. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 36 hours per academic year or 48 hours per calendar year. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health Program and the Study Abroad Office.

PUBH 300.name: Critical Thinking in Public Health
PUBH 300.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.
PUBH 300.description: Designed to prepare students to make reasoned, intelligent decisions about public health matters by learning about and practicing how to think, read, write, and speak critically. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.

PUBH 301.name: Critical Thinking in Public Health II
PUBH 301.prerequisites: PUBH 300 and admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.
PUBH 301.description: Designed to prepare students to make reasoned, intelligent decisions about public health matters by learning about and practicing how to think, read, write, and speak critically. Part two of a two course sequence. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): PUBH 300 and admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.

PUBH 310.name: Public Health and Global Citizenship
PUBH 310.prerequisites: Admission to the Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or Bachelor of Science in Public Health program. Non-majors who have completed PUBH 110 may enroll with consent of the instructor.
PUBH 310.description: Encourages a critical examination of the political, religious, social and economic influences on the continuum of local/global health and the role citizenship plays in advocating for the equitable distribution of health across the planet. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or Bachelor of Science in Public Health program. Non-majors who have completed PUBH 110 may enroll with consent of the instructor.

PUBH 320.name: Ecologies of Health and Modern Life
PUBH 320.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.
PUBH 320.description: Human activity has had an impact on the health of people, animals and environments throughout history. This course uses cross-disciplinary frameworks to examine critical issues in public health that can be traced to the impact of modern life. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.

PUBH 330.name: Health Equity and Health Disparities
PUBH 330.prerequisites: PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course.
PUBH 330.description: How and why national and local health disparities in the U.S. occur and for what racial and ethnic groups they are most severe. Course Information: No credit given if the student has credit in SOC 426. Students who completed a version of SOC 426 with a topic other than health disparities may receive credit with permission of the instructor. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course.

PUBH 340.name: Health Literacy
PUBH 340.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.
PUBH 340.description: Trains students to access, analyze, craft, communicate and use health information in ways that are easily understood and serve to improve public health. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Discussion.

PUBH 345.name: Critical Thinking and Public Health Communication
PUBH 345.prerequisites: PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course.
PUBH 345.description: Prepares students to make reasoned, intelligent decisions about public health matters by learning about how to think, read, write, and speak critically and then apply these skills to communicate public health information to a variety of audiences. Course Information: Credit is not given for PUBH 345  if the student has credit in PUBH 300 and PUBH 340. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course.

PUBH 350.name: Health Systems, Policy, and Advocacy
PUBH 350.prerequisites: PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion.
PUBH 350.description: Examines popular perceptions and the reality of how the U.S. Health System is created and evolves. Course Information: Classes will alternate between traditional classroom sessions and guest lectures or off-campus field visits to health system and policy-making experiences. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: PUBH 205 or one additional PUBH course. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture-Discussion.

PUBH 360.name: Local Citizenship and Community Health Initiatives
PUBH 360.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice.
PUBH 360.description: Explore the concept of community health, identify strategies for developing and implementing community health initiatives, and appreciate community building as an overarching approach for health improvement. Course Information: Field work required. This course entails two classroom sessions per week (3 credit hours) and three hours per week of field work by arrangement (1 credit hour). Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice.

PUBH 370.name: Using the Public Health Toolbox
PUBH 370.prerequisites: Senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.
PUBH 370.description: Opportunities to synthesize and apply knowledge/skills from the preceding core courses, working individually and in groups. Course is linked with the baccalaureate project to be implemented in the last semester of the program. Course Information: Field work required. Prerequisite(s): Senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.

PUBH 390.name: Sustainability Internship
PUBH 390.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in US 130; or Grade of C or better in PHYS 116. Open only to sophomores, juniors, and seniors; or consent of the instructor.
PUBH 390.description: Provides an intellectual framework for students to apply classroom learning to hands-on project-based internships that advance the overall mission of greater social, economic and environmental sustainability in the campus community. Course Information: Same as PUBH 390. Students will be placed in internships with UIC departments and units for approximately 8 hours per week. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in US 130; or Grade of C or better in PHYS 116. Open only to sophomores, juniors, and seniors; or consent of the instructor.

PUBH 393.name: Public Health Fieldwork
PUBH 393.prerequisites: Senior standing in the BA or BS in Public Health program or consent of the School of Public Health Undergraduate Fieldwork Developer.
PUBH 393.description: Public Health fieldwork is an integrative practicum experience for students who are undergraduate public health majors. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. Field work required. Locations are chosen from an array of public health sites established through the SPH Undergraduate Fieldwork Developer. Students are expected to spend a minimum of 90 hours in the field site. Fieldwork schedules may be configured to the mutual benefit of sites and students. Prerequisite(s): Senior standing in the BA or BS in Public Health program or consent of the School of Public Health Undergraduate Fieldwork Developer.

PUBH 394.name: Advanced Special Topics in Public Health
PUBH 394.prerequisites: Admission to the BA or BS in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.
PUBH 394.description: Intensive analysis of selected public health challenges and issues. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the BA or BS in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.

PUBH 395.name: Public Health Seminar III
PUBH 395.prerequisites: Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program and junior standing.
PUBH 395.description: Provides opportunities to communicate skills, tools, and content relevant to students’ development outside of the regular curriculum. Topics may change depending on student needs, interests, and current events. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program and junior standing.

PUBH 396.name: Independent Study in Public Health
PUBH 396.prerequisites: Consent of the instructor supervising the independent study and approval of the department/school.
PUBH 396.description: Self-directed study of a special public health topic under the guidance of a faculty member. Course Information: May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor supervising the independent study and approval of the department/school.

PUBH 397.name: Baccalaureate Project in Public Health
PUBH 397.prerequisites: PUBH 370 and Credit or concurrent registration in PUBH 411 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.
PUBH 397.description: Implementation and presentation of an integrative project designed to demonstrate students' mastery of the overarching curriculum goals for the undergraduate major in public health; demonstration of critical thinking and analytic skills. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. Course will meet weekly. The final will entail up to a full day of presentations of the student projects to peers, faculty, and stakeholders. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 370 and Credit or concurrent registration in PUBH 411 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program.

PUBH 399.name: Research in Public Health Sciences - Undergraduate
PUBH 399.prerequisites: Approval of the department or school and consent of the instructor supervising the research experience.
PUBH 399.description: Research experience under the guidance of a faculty member who will act as a research supervisor. Course Information: Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. May be repeated. Application of credit toward the degree is contingent upon the approval of the student's college and/or department. Previously listed as IPHS 399. Prerequisite(s): Approval of the department or school and consent of the instructor supervising the research experience.

PUBH 410.name: Historical and Contemporary Public Health Challenges I
PUBH 410.prerequisites: Senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.
PUBH 410.description: Part one of a two course sequence, historical and contemporary case studies of social movement(s) will be examined to demonstrate the inter-relatedness of social justice, science/medicine, culture, politics, geography and the public's health. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): Senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.

PUBH 411.name: Historical and Contemporary Public Health Challenges II
PUBH 411.prerequisites: PUBH 410 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.
PUBH 411.description: Part two of a two course sequence, historical and contemporary case studies of social movement(s) will be examined to demonstrate the inter-relatedness of social justice, science/medicine, culture, politics, geography and the public's health. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): PUBH 410 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of the instructor.

PUBH 415.name: Historical and Contemporary Public Health Challenges
PUBH 415.prerequisites: PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: One additional PUBH course.
PUBH 415.description: Historical and contemporary case studies of social movements will be examined to demonstrate the inter-relatedness of social justice, science/medicine, culture, politics, geography, and the public's health. Course Information: Credit is not given for PUBH 415 if the student has credit in  PUBH 410 and PUBH 411. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 100; and PUBH 110 or PUBH 120. Recommended background: One additional PUBH course.

PUBH 420.name: Public Health Problem Solving: Epidemiology in Action!
PUBH 420.prerequisites: PUBH 120 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of instructor.
PUBH 420.description: Through student driven discourse, hands-on projects and instructor facilitated case studies, this course is designed to hone previously learned epidemiological principles and apply them to simulated field conditions. Prerequisite(s): PUBH 120 and senior standing in the Bachelor of Arts in Public Health or the Bachelor of Science in Public Health program or consent of instructor.

PPOL 100.name: Individual Action and Democratic Citizenship
PPOL 100.description: Seeks to enhance understanding of the role of the individual as democratic citizen in shaping policy issues and outcomes in today’s world. Course information: Previously listed as PA 100. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion/Recitation. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

PPOL 101.name: Contemporary Issues in Public Policy
PPOL 101.description: Examines contemporary policy issues and provides students with the tools to identify and analyze information on the past and current policy making and the role of individual and institutional factors in the policy making process. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 105.name: Programming and Data Analysis for Public Policy I
PPOL 105.description: Introduces students to key programming skills in Excel and R for data management, data visualization, and descriptive statistical analysis for policy making.

PPOL 196.name: Special Topics: Public Policy
PPOL 196.description: Special topics in public policy such as: nonprofit management, racial equity analysis in public administration, deliberative democracy and civic analytics.

PPOL 205.name: Programming and Data Analysis for Public Policy II
PPOL 205.description: Provides students with the data, programming, and inferential statistical skills needed to inform basic policy and management decision making. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 105.

PPOL 210.name: Introduction to Public Policy
PPOL 210.description: Introduces students to public policy and the policy-making process. Course Information: Previously listed PA 210. UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 212.name: Evidence-Based Public Policy
PPOL 212.description: Discusses the role of evidence in the policy process in various policy domains such as health, fiscal, educational, and environmental. It covers different types of evidence, including experimental, statistical, and qualitative evidence. UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 220.name: Introduction to Civic Technology
PPOL 220.description: Introduces students to smart cities, open data, government transparency, ethics, hacktivism, digital inclusion, civic innovation, and data-driven decision making. UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 230.name: Nonprofit Organizations and Civil Society
PPOL 230.description: Introduction to the US nonprofit sector and examines the social, political, and economic impact of these organizations in American society from roughly 1870 to today. Historical emphasis on events that have shaped the modern American nonprofit sector. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 230. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 231.name: Social Policy and Inequality in the U
PPOL 231.description: Provides a basic introduction to social policy and inequality in the United States, examines the history of social welfare policies and programs in the US, and explores American cultural values and attitudes. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 231. UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 232.name: International Development Policy
PPOL 232.description: Provides a basic introduction to globalization and international development, and how public policy can shape and address them. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 232. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

PPOL 240.name: Environmental Policy
PPOL 240.description: Provides an introduction to political, economic and social dimensions of environmental policy and teaches methods needed to understand, evaluate, design and implement environment policies, especially in the United States. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 240. UIC GE US Society course.

PPOL 296.name: Special Topics in Public Policy
PPOL 296.description: Special topics in public policy such as: nonprofit management, racial equity analysis in public administration, deliberative democracy and civic analytics.

PPOL 300.name: Urban Policy and Metropolitan Governance
PPOL 300.prerequisites: Admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies and college-level Microeconomics; or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 300.description: Basic structure of the policy process within the urban context including the nature of urban policy, its formulation, evaluation, and implementation. Emphasis on state and local policy development as it relates to urban areas. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 300. Field work required. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies and college-level Microeconomics; or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 303.name: Policy Implementation and Management
PPOL 303.prerequisites: PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 303.description: Policy implementation, management, organizing, budgeting, finance, leadership, motivation, economic development, accountability, ethics, legal and constitutional foundations. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 303. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 305.name: Policy Analysis
PPOL 305.prerequisites: PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 305.description: Policy analysis and approaches to assessing policy problems and alternatives in the U.S. context. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 305. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 307.name: Policy Analysis III: Identifying and Developing Alternatives
PPOL 307.prerequisites: Grade of C or better in ENGL 161; and PPOL 210.
PPOL 307.description: Policy analysis and approaches to assessing policy problems and alternatives in the U.S. context. Course Information: Previously listed as PPOL 211. Prerequisite(s): Grade of C or better in ENGL 161; and PPOL 210.

PPOL 309.name: Public Budgeting and Financial Management
PPOL 309.prerequisites: PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 309.description: Government budgeting, financial, management and the reciprocal relationship between government and the economy. Course Information: Same as US 309. Previously listed as PA 309. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 210 and admission to the BA in Public Policy or Urban Studies; or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 330.name: Introduction to Managing Public and Nonprofit Organizations
PPOL 330.description: Provides an introduction to managing public organizations and the public aspects of nonprofit organizations. Examines the context of public and nonprofit management and consider ways to evaluate and understand organizations. Course Information: Prerequisite(s): PPOL 230.

PPOL 396.name: Special Topics in Public Policy
PPOL 396.description: Special topics in public policy such as: nonprofit management, racial equity analysis in public administration, deliberative democracy and civic analytics. Course Information: Recommended Background: PPOL 100 and PPOL 210.

PPOL 405.name: Evaluating Public Policies and Programs
PPOL 405.description: Designed to introduce students to research methods used in the social sciences to evaluate public programs. There is an overview of evaluation, understanding why we do evaluations and theories impacting evaluations and research design.  Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as PA 405. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 205.

PPOL 430.name: Fundraising and Development for Nonprofit Organizations
PPOL 430.description: Designed to help students learn why and how US nonprofit organizations secure philanthropy through charitable giving. Students will be exposed to knowledge, strategies, best practice principles and scholarly research. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): PPOL 230.

PPOL 467.name: Civic Engagement
PPOL 467.prerequisites: Admission to the MPA program or consent of instructor.
PPOL 467.description: Analytic frameworks and practical strategies for utilizing public participation in government and community decisions. Investigates interactions between civic participation, efforts to improve administration of programs and develop new public policies. Course Information: Same as PA 467. 3 undergraduate hours; 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the MPA program or consent of instructor.

PPOL 490.name: Field Experience in Public Policy and Administration
PPOL 490.prerequisites: Graduate standing required and admission to the MPA program or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 490.description: Students work in an organization such as a government agency, community group, or nonprofit organization. Students are required to submit written work and meet with professor on periodic basis to review work experience. Course Information: Same as PA 490. Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. May be repeated. A maximum of 4 hours of credit may be applied to the Master of Public Administration program. Field work required. Students who have no prior work experience in the public or non-profit sectors are strongly recommended to register for this course. Prerequisite(s): Graduate standing required and admission to the MPA program or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 491.name: Internship Experience in Public Policy and Urban Studies
PPOL 491.prerequisites: Sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 491.description: Students are required to do practical experience through an internship or job placement approved by the College of Urban Planning and Public Affairs. Course Information: Same as US 491. Satisfactory/Unsatisfactory grading only. May be repeated. Previously listed as UPA 491. Field work required. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor.

PPOL 496.name: Special Topics in Public Policy
PPOL 496.description: Special topics in public policy, such as: nonprofit management, racial equity analysis in public administration, deliberative democracy and civic analytics. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Recommended Background: PPOL 100; and PPOL 210.

PPOL 499.name: Independent Study in Public Policy
PPOL 499.prerequisites: Admission to the BA in Public Policy program or consent of the instructor.
PPOL 499.description: Provides an opportunity for students to pursue an independent project that is not available through the required Public Policy coursework. Course Information: Previously listed as PA 499. May be repeated for a maximum of 6 hours. Prerequisite(s): Admission to the BA in Public Policy program or consent of the instructor.

PPA 401.name: Data Analysis I
PPA 401.prerequisites: POLS 200 and POLS 201; or graduate standing.
PPA 401.description: Statistical inference for the social sciences. Emphasis on univariate and bivariate statistics. Course Information: Same as POLS 401. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): POLS 200 and POLS 201; or graduate standing.

RES 250.name: Real Estate Principles
RES 250.description: Introduction to real estate, including principles of finance, investments, accounting, management, marketing, and statistic.  Provides background necessary for additional courses in real estate.

RES 420.name: Real Estate Market Analysis
RES 420.description: Analysis of real property markets, including land, buildings, and improvements. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): ECON 120.

RES 425.name: Real Estate Valuation
RES 425.description: Introduction to the valuation of real estate, with focus on the underlying determinants of location value. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): ECON 120.

RES 440.name: International Real Estate Markets
RES 440.description: Survey and analysis of issues related to international real estate markets, with an emphasis on North American and Asia. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): ECON 120.

RES 450.name: Real Estate Data Analysis
RES 450.description: Analysis of real estate data using statistical software programs, with an emphasis on housing data. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): ECON 120.

RES 460.name: Commercial Real Estate
RES 460.description: Fundamentals of the commercial real estate, including mortgages and investment analysis. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): FIN 300.

RES 470.name: Real Estate Investments
RES 470.description: Introduction to investment in real estate, with focus on types of investment and analysis of investment performance. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Extensive computer use required. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): FIN 300.

RES 472.name: Real Estate Finance
RES 472.description: Finance principles applied to real estate; financing of residential and income-producing real estate; real estate development finance; secondary mortgage market; taxation and real estate finance. Course Information: Same as FIN 472. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as ECON 472. May not be used to satisfy the economics credit requirement for the MA in Economics and PhD in Economics. Only elective credit will be applied toward these degrees. Graduate students will complete a term project to meet the additional credit hour requirement. Prerequisite(s): FIN 300.

RES 494.name: Topics in Real Estate
RES 494.prerequisites: RES 250 and FIN 300; or FIN 301 and FIN 302.
RES 494.description: An intensive study of a selected topic in real estate. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Students may register for more than one section per term. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours for undergraduates; may be repeated to a maximum of 8 hours for graduate students. Prerequisite(s): RES 250 and FIN 300; or FIN 301 and FIN 302.

RES 499.name: Research Experience
RES 499.prerequisites: Consent of the instructor and Department Head.
RES 499.description: Independent student research experience under the supervision of a faculty member. The faculty member and student will determine a specific, discipline appropriate research project to complete within the semester and submit a written report. Course Information: Extensive computer use required. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor and Department Head.

RELS 100.name: Religion in Human Experience
RELS 100.description: Introduction to basic questions about religion, focusing on the theological and philosophical ways in which humans have sought to understand faith, transcendence, and the nature of god(s). UIC GE Individual and Society course.

RELS 101.name: Introduction to World Religions
RELS 101.description: A historical survey of the origins and development of several of the world's largest religions, including but not limited to Buddhism, Hinduism, Judaism, Christianity, and Islam. UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 106.name: What Is Religion? 3 hours
RELS 106.description: Examination of issues concerning religion, including varying views of the purposes that religions serve, differences between religions, and arguments for the existence of God. Course Information: Same as PHIL 106. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

RELS 117.name: Understanding the Holocaust
RELS 117.description: Examines the Holocaust of European Jewry as the result of antisemitic ideology and the development of modern German political forces; implementation of the Final Solution. Course Information: Same as HIST 117 and JST 117. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE Past course.

RELS 120.name: Catholic Thought: An Introduction
RELS 120.description: Introduction to the main topics, interests, and methods of Catholic thought. Course Information: Same as CST 120. UIC GE Past course.

RELS 121.name: Introduction to Judaism
RELS 121.description: Introduction to the Jewish religion as it has developed from its roots in the Hebrew Bible until today, emphasizing its central texts and concepts. Same as JST 101. UIC GE Individual and Society course.

RELS 122.name: Minority Perspectives in the Germanic Context
RELS 122.description: Investigation of the challenges and/or opportunities of multicultural societies by examining in a socio-historical context texts created by members of Europe's ethnic, religious, and national minorities. Course Information: Same as GER 122 and JST 122. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Lectures, discussion, and readings in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 123.name: Introduction to Yiddish Culture and Literature
RELS 123.description: Yiddish culture in Europe and the U.S. in socio-historical context. Focus on the role of Yiddish in conceptions of secular, cultural, religious, national Jewish identities. Course Information: Same as GER 123 and JST 123. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Lectures, discussion, and readings in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 124.name: Introduction to the Hebrew Bible
RELS 124.description: A study of the Five Books of Moses (a.k.a Torah or Pentateuch) within the contexts of the ancient Near East and biblical literature.  Course Information:  Same as CL 124 and JST 124.  Taught in English. UIC GE Past course.

RELS 125.name: Black Religious Traditions
RELS 125.description: Introduction to the religious history of black people in the United States and the diaspora. Examination of religious institutions, the role of religion in black social and political movements, mass rebellions, and revolts. Course Information: Same as BLST 125. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.

RELS 126.name: Introduction to Jewish History
RELS 126.description: Overview of Jewish history from Biblical times until the present. Topics will range from second Temple Judea and the beginning of the diaspora through 21st-century Jewish life in America and Israel. Course Information: Same as JST 102. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE Past course.

RELS 127.name: Diaspora, Exile, Genocide: Aspects of the European Jewish Experience in Literature and Film
RELS 127.description: Literature and films on European Jewish responses to anti-Semitism and persecution in a historical context to reveal the condition of post-Enlightenment German-speaking Jewish and Yiddish-speaking societies. Course Information: Same as GER 125 and JST 125. No credit toward a major or minor program offered by the Department of Germanic Studies. Taught in English. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 128.name: The Jesus Movement and the New Testament
RELS 128.description: Investigates the Jesus movement from its beginnings as a small Jewish reform sect through its development into the religion known as Christianity. Uses New Testament writings as primary sources for reconstructing the past. Course Information: Same as CL 128 and CST 128. UIC GE Past course.

RELS 130.name: Introduction to Islam
RELS 130.description: Introductory study of the religion, culture, and present variety of Islam in Islamic countries and in the West. UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 150.name: Catholicism in U
RELS 150.description: The Catholic experience in the United States from its colonial origins to the present. Course Information: Same as CST 150 and HIST 150. UIC GE US Society course.

RELS 175.name: Understanding the Bible as Literature
RELS 175.description: A broad overview of various literary genres in the Bible such as origin narrative, historical narrative, poetry, wisdom literature, prophetic/apocalyptic literature, parable, and epistle. Course Information: Same as ENGL 175. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

RELS 177.name: Middle Eastern Civilization
RELS 177.description: Introduction to the culture and society of the Middle East, with special attention to the development of Islam and the consequences of westernization. Course Information: Same as HIST 177. Course is offered in both face-to-face and hybrid/ online formats. Check the class schedule for details. When taught online or hybrid, students will be required to have reliable internet access and a means for accessing it (computer preferable). Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 208.name: Classical Mythology
RELS 208.description: Intensive study of the gods and heroic sagas of the cultures of the ancient Mediterranean world, through original sources in translation. Course Information: Same as CL 208. All readings are in English. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE Past course.

RELS 215.name: Anthropology of Religion
RELS 215.prerequisites: ENGL 161. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
RELS 215.description: Exploration of varieties of religious experience, including magic and witchcraft, with emphasis on non-Western religions and the role of religious institutions in social integration. Course Information: Same as ANTH 215. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 220.name: Buddhist Art and Architecture
RELS 220.description: A chronologically and thematically organized survey of Buddhist art and architecture in Asia and beyond. Course Information: Same as AH 220 and GLAS 220. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 222.name: Phil in the Islamic World
RELS 222.prerequisites: One non-javascript:;logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor.
RELS 222.description: Survey of philosophy in the Islamic world from 8th century until today. Topics include: logic and psychology to metaphysics, theology, and politics. Special emphasis on cultural interactions between philosophers of different faiths and cultures. Course Information: Same as ARAB 222 and PHIL 222. Prerequisite(s): One non-javascript:;logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor.

RELS 225.name: Topics in Muslim-Jewish Relations
RELS 225.description: Muslim-Jewish interactions from the rise of Islam until contemporary times, the relationship between Biblical and Quranic materials, the Jewish and Islamic interpretive tradition and the legal systems of the two religious traditions.  Course Information:  Same as CL 225 and JST 225.

RELS 230.name: Topics in Islam
RELS 230.description: Topics, issues, and methodologies in Islamic studies. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Recommended background: ENGL 160. UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 231.name: Topics in Jewish Thought
RELS 231.description: Survey of major topics in Jewish theology, philosophy, ethics and political thought. Course Information: Same as JST 230. May be repeated if topics vary. Recommended Background: JST 101 or the equivalent.

RELS 232.name: The Religious World of the Earliest Christians
RELS 232.description: Explores the religious world of the earliest Christians, focusing on the ways in which early Christian ideas and practices arose out of the cultures, religions, and philosophies of the ancient Mediterranean basin. Course Information: Same as CL 232 and HIST 232. UIC GE Past course.

RELS 233.name: Topics in Religious Studies
RELS 233.description: Study of a specific topic in religious studies. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary.

RELS 240.name: Philosophy and Revelation: Jewish and Christian Perspectives
RELS 240.prerequisites: One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.
RELS 240.description: Introduction to philosophical ways of addressing the claim that a book (the Bible, the Quran) comes from God.  Texts by Immanuel Kant, Moses Mendelssohn, and Soren Kierkegaard, among others.  Course Information:  Previously listed as RELS 141. Same as PHIL 240 and JST 240.  Prerequisite(s): One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 241.name: Philosophy of Religion
RELS 241.prerequisites: One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture.
RELS 241.description: Philosophical inquiry into the grounds of faith and belief, the nature of religious and mystical experience, and the existence and nature of God. Course Information: Same as PHIL 241. Prerequisite(s): One non-logic course in philosophy or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion and one Lecture.

RELS 242.name: The History of Jewish Biblical Interpretation
RELS 242.description: Jewish interpretation of the Hebrew bible. A survey of the span of Jewish history and the wide range of cultural contexts that have impacted the understanding of the Torah.  Course Information:  Same as CL 242 and JST 242. UIC GE Past course.

RELS 243.name: The Archaeology of Buddhism
RELS 243.description: The development, spread, integration, and decline of Buddhism in many parts of Asia. The study of material culture as a way of reconstructing and understanding the religious past. Course Information: Same as ANTH 243. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161.

RELS 246.name: Sociology of Religion
RELS 246.prerequisites: One social sciences Course Distribution Credit  (CDC) course and sophomore standing. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.
RELS 246.description: Analysis of the structures and functions of religious institutions in modern society.Special attention to the interplay between religion and other social phenomena, such as economics, politics, and secular culture. Course Information: Same as SOC 246. Occasional field trips. Prerequisite(s): One social sciences Course Distribution Credit  (CDC) course and sophomore standing. Class Schedule Information:  To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture-Discussion and one Practice. UIC GE Individual and Society course, and UIC GE US Society course.

RELS 250.name: Eastern and Western Philosophies of Religion
RELS 250.description: Eastern and Western philosophies of religion: Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, and Christianity. Course Information: Same as INST 250. UIC GE World Cultures course.

RELS 255.name: Religious Diversity: Conceptual and Practical Issues
RELS 255.description: The facts of religious diversity and the questions raised by them. Special attention to Catholic Christian perspectives, treating them from secular and other religious perspectives.

RELS 256.name: Religion in American History
RELS 256.prerequisites: ENGL 161. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion/Recitation. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.
RELS 256.description: A survey of the varieties of religious experience in American history from the sixteenth to the twentieth-first centuries, with emphasis on group formation and the social and cultural consequences of American religious diversity. Course Information: Same as HIST 256. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 161. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion/Recitation. UIC GE Past course, and UIC GE US Society course.

RELS 257.name: Religious Diversity in the Healthcare Fields
RELS 257.description: Explores the intersection of religion and healthcare with the goal of preparing future healthcare providers to engage with diverse religious perspectives and behaviors in healthcare settings.

RELS 279.name: The Qur'an: A Historical and Cultural Approach
RELS 279.description: Introduction to the text, history of interpretation, and the cultural significance of the Qur'an. Course Information: Previously listed as RELS 330. Taught in English. Recommended background: RELS 130.

RELS 292.name: Major Religious Thinkers
RELS 292.description: An examination of one or more major/classical thinkers and their writings. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Previously listed as RELS 320. Prerequisite(s): RELS 100, 101, 115, 215, or 241.

RELS 293.name: Dante's Divine Comedy
RELS 293.description: Love. Justice. Redemption. In this class, we journey with a medieval poet whose timeless vision brings sinners, saints, and seekers to life—and reveals what it means to be human. Course Information: Same as CL 293 and ITAL 293. Previously listed as RELS 193. Taught in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE Past course.

RELS 294.name: Topics in Catholic History
RELS 294.description: An investigation of the impact of human migration and cultural pluralism on Catholicism and an analysis of the role of the Catholic Church in group relations. Topics will vary. Course Information: Same as CST 294 and HIST 294. May be repeated if topics vary.

RELS 295.name: Topics in Catholic Thought
RELS 295.description: Critical investigation of a topic or topics central to the development of Catholic thought, carried on by study of its proponents and opponents. Topics will vary. Course Information: Same as CST 295. May be repeated if topics vary.

RELS 296.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
RELS 296.description: How Jews became a modern ethnic group, how their experiences compare with other ethnic groups, and how their experiences in modern times vary from nation to nation. Course Information: Same as JST 296. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours.

RELS 311.name: Gender and Sexuality in Early Christianity and Judaism
RELS 311.description: Examination of the root of contemporary perspectives on gender and sexuality in the early traditions of Judaism and Christianity including the Bible, the Epic of Gilgamesh, the Church Fathers, the Talmud, and legends of the saints.  Course Information:  Same as GWS 311 and JST 311.

RELS 337.name: Latino/a Religions in the US
RELS 337.description: How Latinx communities venerate ancestors and deities, create and remake sacred space, practice healing, and organize politically through the lens of of documentaries, fiction and literature, visual art, and ethnographic studies. Course Information: Same as  LALS 337.

RELS 376.name: Literature and Religion
RELS 376.description: Studies in the relation of literature to doctrines, imagery, practices, experiences, or history of one or more religious traditions. Course Information:  Same as ENGL 376. Previously listed as RELS 343. Recommended background: Any of ENGL 101, 105, 175, or 207-209.

RELS 392.name: Major Problems in Religious Studies
RELS 392.prerequisites: At least one course in religious studies.
RELS 392.description: In-depth examination of a major topic or problem in religious thought. Topics will vary. Course Information: May be repeated if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): At least one course in religious studies.

RELS 394.name: Topics in Catholic History and Culture
RELS 394.prerequisites: One course in history or Catholic studies; or consent of the instructor.
RELS 394.description: Exploration of various topics in Catholic history and culture. Course Information: Same as CST 394 and HIST 394. Prerequisite(s): One course in history or Catholic studies; or consent of the instructor.

RELS 395.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
RELS 395.description: Selected topics in Jewish culture and history. Course Information: Same as JST 394. May be repeated to a maximum of 9 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor.

RELS 399.name: Independent Study
RELS 399.prerequisites: Sophomore standing or above; and consent of the instructor. Recommended Background: Two courses in religious studies.
RELS 399.description: Selected topics for individual study. Course Information:  May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Students may register in more than one section per term.  Prerequisite(s): Sophomore standing or above; and consent of the instructor. Recommended Background: Two courses in religious studies.

RELS 420.name: Advanced Studies in Jewish Religion
RELS 420.prerequisites: JST 101; and JST 230 or JST 242 or JST 254.
RELS 420.description: In-depth study of a period or mode of Jewish religious development or textual production, or an examination of a religious tenet or practice across various historical periods. Topics will vary.Course Information: Same as JST 420. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated up to 1 time(s) if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): JST 101; and JST 230 or JST 242 or JST 254.

RELS 422.name: Studies in Medieval Philosophy
RELS 422.prerequisites: One non-logic 200-level philosophy course; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture.
RELS 422.description: Study of selected philosophers such as Boethius, Avicenna, Aquinas, William Ockham, and selected issues such as the relationship between faith and reason, the nature of reason, the nature of human cognition, the nature of morality. Course Information: Same as PHIL 422. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated if topics or figures vary. Approval to repeat course granted by the department. Prerequisite(s): One non-logic 200-level philosophy course; or consent of the instructor. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Discussion/Recitation and one Lecture.

RELS 430.name: Advanced Studies in Jewish Thought
RELS 430.prerequisites: One 100-level and one 200-level course in Jewish Studies.
RELS 430.description: Advanced topics in Jewish theology, philosophy, ethics or political thought. Course Information: Same as JST 430. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): One 100-level and one 200-level course in Jewish Studies.

RELS 441.name: Topics in Philosophy of Religion
RELS 441.prerequisites: One non-logic 200-level course in philosophy; or consent of the instructor.
RELS 441.description: Intensive study of one or more selected topics concerning the philosophical aspects of basic religious beliefs and concepts. Course Information: Same as PHIL 441. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated if topics vary. Approval to repeat course granted by the department. Prerequisite(s): One non-logic 200-level course in philosophy; or consent of the instructor.

RELS 446.name: Race, Ethnicity, and Gender in American Religion
RELS 446.prerequisites: SOC 100 and junior standing or above; or consent of instructor.
RELS 446.description: Religious institutions in the U.S. as a crucible for racial, ethnic, and gender identities, group formation, and intergroup relations; major world religions represented in the U.S. Course Information: Same as SOC 446. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): SOC 100 and junior standing or above; or consent of instructor.

RELS 475.name: The Bible as Literature: Hebrew Bible
RELS 475.description: Literary analysis of genres and themes of the Hebrew Bible and close reading of the biblical texts. Sources of the Bible and their historical context. Course Information: Same as ENGL 475 and JST 475. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as RELS 478. Taught in English. Recommended background: Any of ENGL 175 or ENGL 207-209.

RELS 476.name: The Bible as Literature: New Testament
RELS 476.description: Literary analysis of the New Testament and its historical and religious contexts, focusing on the gospels and Pauline letters. Course Information: Same as ENGL 476. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Previously listed as RELS 479. Recommended background: Any of ENGL 175, 207-209, 475.

RELS 494.name: Topics in Jewish Studies
RELS 494.prerequisites: JST 101 or JST 102 or consent of the instructor.
RELS 494.description: Selected topics in Jewish studies. Course Information: Same as JST 494. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours if topics vary.  Prerequisite(s): JST 101 or JST 102 or consent of the instructor.

RELS 495.name: Topics in Religious History
RELS 495.prerequisites: 3 hours of history or consent of the instructor.
RELS 495.description: Specific topics are announced each term. Course Information: Same as HIST 495. 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated. Students may register in more than one section per term. Prerequisite(s): 3 hours of history or consent of the instructor.

RUSS 101.name: Elementary Russian I
RUSS 101.prerequisites: For students who have had no formal work in Russian.
RUSS 101.description: Basics of Russian grammar and vocabulary. Reading and discussing the culture and modern life of Russia. Course Information: Taught in a blended format. Internet access is required. A high-speed connection is strongly recommended. Prerequisite(s): For students who have had no formal work in Russian.

RUSS 102.name: Elementary Russian II
RUSS 102.prerequisites: RUSS 101; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
RUSS 102.description: Continues RUSS 101. Course Information: This class is taught in a blended format. Internet access is required. A high-speed connection is strongly recommended. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 101; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

RUSS 103.name: Intermediate Russian I
RUSS 103.prerequisites: RUSS 102; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
RUSS 103.description: Futher study of Russian grammar and vocabulary. Reading and discussing the culture and modern life of Russia. Introduction to expository, persuasive and argumentative styles. Course Information: This class is taught in a blended format. Internet access is required. A high-speed connection is strongly recommended. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 102; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

RUSS 104.name: Intermediate Russian II
RUSS 104.prerequisites: RUSS 103; or appropriate score on the department placement test. IAI GECC Humanities course.
RUSS 104.description: Continuation of RUSS 103. Futher study of Russian grammar and vocabulary. Reading and discussing the culture and modern life of Russia. Introduction to expository, persuasive and argumentative styles Course Information: This class is taught in a blended format. Internet access is required. A high-speed connection is strongly recommended. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 103; or appropriate score on the department placement test. IAI GECC Humanities course.

RUSS 115.name: Russian Culture Before the Revolution
RUSS 115.description: The main trends of Russian thought and manners from the beginning to the Revolution: literature, philosophy, religion, art, architecture, intellectual life. Audio-visual emphasis. IAI GECC Humanities course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 116.name: Russian Culture: The Soviet Period
RUSS 116.description: The transformation of Russian culture after 1917: literature, art, architecture, philosophy, intellectual trends; emphasis on the ideology of Socialist Realism. Audio-visual emphasis. IAI GECC Humanities course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 120.name: The Russian Short Story in Translation
RUSS 120.description: Introduction to important Russian short stories of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries; the elements of fiction; close analysis of literary texts. IAI GECC Humanities course, and UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 130.name: Masterpieces of Russian Literature in Translation
RUSS 130.description: Introduction to Russian novellas and novels of the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. IAI GECC Humanities course, and UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 140.name: Understanding the Body: Health, Gender, and Disability in Russian Culture
RUSS 140.description: Introduction to the theoretical understanding and representation of the body through examples of Russian literature and arts. Course explores such topics as illness, health, reproduction, disability, gender, and power. Course Information: Taught in English. Extensive computer use required. No knowledge of Russian language or culture required. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 150.name: Introduction to Russian Cinema
RUSS 150.description: Introduction to major themes, trends, and techniques of Russian/Soviet cinema. Explores film as an expression of social reality and political ideology. Comparisons drawn with American cinema. Course Information: Taught in English. Films screened with English subtitles. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 201.name: Advanced Russian through Media and Film
RUSS 201.prerequisites: RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
RUSS 201.description: Development of advanced language skills through the study of Russian media and film from a variety of periods. In-depth analysis of past and present Russian cultural artifacts. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

RUSS 202.name: Advanced Russian through Contemporary Culture
RUSS 202.prerequisites: RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
RUSS 202.description: Development of skills in spoken and written Russian, within the context of contemporary Russian culture, via a variety of sources, such as: news articles, comics, music. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

RUSS 203.name: Advanced Russian through Short Stories
RUSS 203.prerequisites: RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.
RUSS 203.description: Development of advanced language skills: writing, reading, listening comprehension, and speaking. Exploration and analysis of Russian cultural tropes within the context of short stories. Course Information: May be repeated for a maximum of 6 hours. May be repeated if topics vary. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 104; or appropriate score on the department placement test.

RUSS 239.name: Twentieth-Century Russia in Film
RUSS 239.prerequisites: ENGL 160 or completion of any 100-level history course. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course.
RUSS 239.description: Survey of social, political and cultural development of the Russian Empire and Soviet Union through the use of film as a historical source. Course Information: Same as HIST 239 and MOVI 239. Prerequisite(s): ENGL 160 or completion of any 100-level history course. Class Schedule Information: To be properly registered, students must enroll in one Lecture and one Discussion. UIC GE Past course.

RUSS 241.name: Dostoyevsky
RUSS 241.description: Selected short stories and novels. Course Information: Taught in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

RUSS 242.name: Tolstoy
RUSS 242.description: Discussion of selected short stories and plays. Course Information: Taught in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

RUSS 244.name: Women in Russian Literature
RUSS 244.description: Major works by and about women in Russian literature: experiences of women and societal attitudes toward them. Course Information: Same as GWS 244. Taught in English. UIC GE Creative Arts course, and UIC GE World Cultures course.

RUSS 247.name: Literature and Fantasy in Russia
RUSS 247.prerequisites: Sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Creative Arts course.
RUSS 247.description: Fantasy as a literary genre. Comparison of the artistic strategies employed by works of literature and film into which the fantastic intrudes in the form of alternate states of consciousness such as dream, inebriation, and madness. Course Information: Taught in English. Prerequisite(s): Sophomore standing or above; or consent of the instructor. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

RUSS 248.name: Russian Visual and Material Culture
RUSS 248.prerequisites: Completion of ENGL 160; or any 100-level RUSS or AH course. UIC GE Creative Arts course.
RUSS 248.description: Non-textual manifestations of Russian culture such as painting, sculpture, architecture, design and decorative arts in a way that emphasizes how an object's compositional medium relates to its aesthetic and functional purposes. Course Information: Same as AH 248. Taught in English. Prerequisite(s): Completion of ENGL 160; or any 100-level RUSS or AH course. UIC GE Creative Arts course.

RUSS 321.name: Great Russian 19th-Century Novels and the European Tradition
RUSS 321.prerequisites: Junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor.
RUSS 321.description: Reading and interpretation of various 19th-century novels, including, but not limited to, the works of Pushkin, Lermontov, Gogol, Turgenev, Dostoevsky, and Tolstoy. Emphasis on the socio-historic and intellectual context of these works. Course Information: Taught in English. Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor.

RUSS 330.name: Art and Politics: Soviet and Post-Soviet Literature
RUSS 330.description: An examination of various Russian literary and aesthetic practices with a focus on how writers defined their art vis-à-vis its contribution to or autonomy from the ideological goals of the Soviet state. Course Information: Taught in English.

RUSS 399.name: Independent Study
RUSS 399.prerequisites: Junior standing, consent of the instructor and the head of the department. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.
RUSS 399.description: Investigation of special problems under the general direction of a staff member. Course Information: May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours. Prerequisite(s): Junior standing, consent of the instructor and the head of the department. Class Schedule Information: This course counts toward the limited number of independent study hours accepted toward the degree and the major.

RUSS 410.name: Structure of Modern Russian
RUSS 410.prerequisites: RUSS 201; and credit or concurrent registration in RUSS 202; or consent of the instructor.
RUSS 410.description: Introduction to Russian morphology and phonology. Examination of the Russian language as a system with particular attention to verb conjugation and noun declension, with the goal of improving students’ practical language skills. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. Prerequisite(s): RUSS 201; and credit or concurrent registration in RUSS 202; or consent of the instructor.

RUSS 440.name: Topics in Russian Culture and Cultural Studies
RUSS 440.prerequisites: Junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor.
RUSS 440.description: Exploration of various topics in Russian culture through an interdisciplinary prism, addressing intersections between visual and verbal arts as well as high and popular culture. Content varies.Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated to a maximum of 6 hours for undergraduate students or 8 hours for graduate students, if topics vary, and with consent of the instructor. Taught in English. All texts are available in English. Students pursuing a major or minor in Russian, or an MA or PhD in Slavic Studies, will be required to read primary texts in the target language. Prerequisite(s): Junior standing or above; or consent of the instructor.

RUSS 460.name: Topics in Russian Literature
RUSS 460.prerequisites: Consent of the instructor Junior, senior, or graduate standing. Or consent of the instructor.
RUSS 460.description: Study of a major author, movement, genre, or special topic. Content varies. Course Information: 3 undergraduate hours. 4 graduate hours. May be repeated up to 2 time(s) if topics vary. Taught in English. Prerequisite(s): Consent of the instructor Junior, senior, or graduate standing. Or consent of the instructor.

RUSS 499.name: Independent Study
